[main 2309cff] Documentation refresh sweeping CLAUDE.md, HANDOFF-TL.md, multiple docs/ files, and inline comments across rustc-lang-facade and toylangc to reflect the post-B6/B7 and post-5c.4 two-crate state. Replaces stale references to retired mechanisms (CODEGEN_SKIP_HOOK, VISIBILITY_OVERRIDE_HOOK, CARGO_INCREMENTAL=0 stopgap, FileLoader/direct mode, generate_stubs injection) with pointers to the partitioner override, the B6 stateless monomorphize_type contract, and the @SMINCZ / @DPSFDOZ / @GCMLZ / @MRRIWMZ arcana. Adds a new docs/reasoning/why-outer-params-suffice.md and rewrites HANDOFF-TL.md as a maintenance-mode handoff (roadmap complete). The only non-doc behavior change is in toylangc/tests/standalone_tests.rs: switches the standalone tests' rustup invocation from the retired rustc-fork toolchain to vanilla nightly-2025-01-15. Bumps the test-count line in CLAUDE.md from 209 to 210.

       1 +//! `toylang.toml` parser.
       2 +//!
       3 +//! Per @MRRIWMZ, this file is parsed twice per build: once by `toylangc
       4 +//! build` orchestrating cargo (via `build::build_project`), and once by
       5 +//! the wrapper-mode child process cargo spawns for the primary crate
       6 +//! (via `main::run_wrapper_mode`). Both sites call `parse` below so any
       7 +//! schema change takes effect on both automatically. Schema changes that
       8 +//! affect the path-resolution semantics (e.g., `[project].source`'s
       9 +//! relative-vs-absolute interpretation) must keep both call sites in
      10 +//! sync — the arcana documents the side-channel invariant.

  Summary

  Facade (rustc-lang-facade/src/):

⏺ rustc Extension Points — A Step-by-Step Guide

  This walks through the rustc hooks this project touches, in roughly the order they fire. It's scoped to what's actually used here; rustc has many more extension points that
  aren't relevant.

  Step 0: The gate — #![feature(rustc_private)]

  All of rustc's internal crates (rustc_driver, rustc_interface, rustc_middle, rustc_codegen_ssa, rustc_codegen_llvm, rustc_monomorphize, ...) are nightly-only and gated by this
  feature. A crate that depends on them declares it at the crate root. There's no SemVer; APIs drift across nightlies. It's the official "all bets off" escape hatch.

  You also need to link against the compiler's shared libraries at runtime. On macOS that means setting DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH to the sysroot's lib/; on Linux it's LD_LIBRARY_PATH.

  Step 1: The driver entry point — rustc_driver::RunCompiler

  rustc_driver::RunCompiler::new(rustc_args, &mut my_callbacks).run();

  rustc_args is a &[String] in rustc argv shape (skip argv[0]; the rest is whatever the rustc CLI would accept). my_callbacks is any type implementing rustc_driver::Callbacks.
  run() drives the whole compilation.

  Small sibling utilities:
  - rustc_driver::install_ice_hook(report_url, |_| {}) — installs a panic hook that prints a "please report at " onICE.
  - rustc_driver::catch_with_exit_code(|| { ... }) — maps rustc's panic/early-exit patterns into a clean process exit code.

  Step 2: The lifecycle hooks — rustc_driver::Callbacks

  trait Callbacks {
      fn config(&mut self, config: &mut Config) {}
      fn after_analysis<'tcx>(&mut self, compiler: &Compiler, tcx: TyCtxt<'tcx>)
          -> Compilation { Compilation::Continue }
      // a few more, all defaulted
  }

  Two methods matter:

  - config fires very early, before parsing. You mutate the Config struct to install query overrides, swap the codegen backend, register a file loader, etc. This is your only
  chance to hook things in before rustc starts real work.
  - after_analysis fires after type-checking / borrow-checking, before monomorphization. You get a TyCtxt<'tcx> andcan walk the HIR, run validation, emit diagnostics. Return
  Compilation::Continue to proceed or Compilation::Stop to halt.

  Step 3: The config struct — rustc_interface::Config

  Two fields are relevant here:

  config.override_queries    = Some(my_override_fn);
  config.make_codegen_backend = Some(Box::new(|_opts| MyBackend::new()));

  Both are callbacks rustc invokes at the right moment during setup. override_queries is a plain fn pointer: fn(&Session, &mut Providers). make_codegen_backend is a boxed closure
  returning Box<dyn CodegenBackend>.

  Step 4: The query provider table — rustc_middle::util::Providers

  Rustc's compilation is a graph of queries — demand-driven, memoized computations each keyed by some input and returning some output. layout_of, symbol_name, optimized_mir,
  fn_abi_of_instance, and hundreds more. They are the backbone of incremental compilation and lazy evaluation.

  Providers is a big struct of fn pointers, one field per query. Rustc populates it with defaults before calling your override. Your override replaces specific fields:

  fn my_override(_sess: &Session, providers: &mut Providers) {
      // Save the default if you want to delegate to it:
      DEFAULT_OPTIMIZED_MIR.set(providers.optimized_mir).ok();
      providers.optimized_mir = my_provider_fn;
  }

  The idiomatic pattern: stash the default in a OnceLock, have your replacement either handle the input itself or fall through to the saved default for inputs you don't care
  about.

  Below are the six queries this project overrides. Each is its own extension point with a distinct key, return type, and firing semantics.

  4a. optimized_mir

  ┌─────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │         │                                                                  │
  ├─────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Key     │ LocalDefId                                                       │
  ├─────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Returns │ &'tcx Body<'tcx> — the optimized MIR for the function            │
  ├─────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Fires   │ during monomorphization, once per function whose MIR is demanded │
  └─────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Return a hand-built MIR body and rustc's mono collector walks whatever you return. Call terminators queue calleesfor monomorphization.
  Rvalue::Cast(CastKind::PointerCoercion(ReifyFnPointer, _)) queues the target Instance for codegen even if it's never called. NullOp::SizeOf queues a type.

  Constructing valid MIR is fiddly:
  - set_required_consts and set_mentioned_items must be called on synthetic bodies (the normal mir_promoted pass doesn't run for them, so the mono collector panics if they're
  unset).
  - Every BasicBlockData needs a terminator. TerminatorKind::Unreachable is valid for bodies that are never executed.
  - TypingEnv::fully_monomorphized() is a typing-mode flag (PostAnalysis + Reveal::All), not an input assertion — bodies containing ty::TyKind::Param placeholders flow through
  cleanly; rustc's collector substitutes them per caller when walking the body.

  4b. symbol_name

  ┌─────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │         │                                                                                        │
  ├─────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Key     │ Instance<'tcx> — a (DefId, GenericArgsRef<'tcx>) bundle with erased late-bound regions │
  ├─────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Returns │ ty::SymbolName<'tcx>                                                                   │
  ├─────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Fires   │ when rustc needs the linker-visible name for a specific monomorphization               │
  └─────────┴────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Return any string via SymbolName::new(tcx, &s). Useful for redirecting mangled names to your own scheme.

  Important subtlety: computing a symbol name does not drive codegen. It's a pure read. If you only hook symbol_name you'll get "undefined symbol" link errors — driving emission
  requires something like ReifyFnPointer in a MIR body (4a).

  4c. layout_of

  ┌─────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │         │                                                           │
  ├─────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Key     │ PseudoCanonicalInput<'tcx, Ty<'tcx>> (on this nightly; older nightlies used ParamEnvAnd)                                                           │
  ├─────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Returns │ Result<TyAndLayout<'tcx>, &'tcx LayoutError<'tcx>>                                                           │
  ├─────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Fires   │ extremely high volume — once for every type whose size/alignment rustc needs, including derived forms(*mut T, &T, Option<T>, tuples containing your type, etc.) │
  └─────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Return a LayoutData describing the type's memory shape. BackendRepr::Memory { sized: true } tells rustc it's an opaque blob — no scalar-pair decomposition, no niche
  optimization.

  Filter aggressively. You want to intercept TyKind::Adt only for specific types; intercepting derived forms corrupts their layouts and ICEs codegen. Check has_param() on the args
   to skip generic definitions that haven't been instantiated yet.

  4d. mir_shims

  ┌─────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │         │      │
  ├─────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Key     │ ty::InstanceKind<'tcx> — variants include DropGlue(DefId, Option<Ty<'tcx>>), FnPtrShim(...), CloneShim(...) │
  ├─────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Returns │ Body<'tcx>      │
  ├─────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Fires   │ when rustc needs a generated shim body      │
  └─────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Pattern-match the InstanceKind and synthesize a MIR body. DropGlue(_, Some(ty)) is the common target — you can return a body that calls a custom destructor.

  4e. collect_and_partition_mono_items

  ┌─────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │         │                                                                                       │
  ├─────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Key     │ () (singleton query)                                                                  │
  ├─────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Returns │ (&'tcx DefIdSet, &'tcx [CodegenUnit<'tcx>]) — the reachable-items set + CGU partition │
  ├─────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Fires   │ once per compilation, the bridge between mono collection and codegen                  │
  └─────────┴───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Delegate to the upstream provider, then reconstruct the returned CGU slice — filter items out, or mutate MonoItemData fields (linkage, visibility) on items that remain. The LLVM
   backend reads data.linkage directly from the CGU slice it receives; it doesn't re-query mono_item_linkage_and_visibility, so post-partition mutations survive to LLVM emission.

  Useful for removing items from rustc's codegen path or forcing specific linkage decisions without a source-level #[linkage] attribute.

  4f. upstream_monomorphizations_for

  ┌─────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │         │                                     │
  ├─────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Key     │ LocalDefId                                     │
  ├─────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Returns │ Option<&'tcx UnordMap<GenericArgsRef<'tcx>, CrateNum>>                                     │
  ├─────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Fires   │ during mono collection when deciding whether a generic monomorphization should be linked from an upstream rlib rather than emitted locally │
  └─────────┴────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Return None to force local emission. Matters when generic items live in an rlib that doesn't contain the specificinstantiation a downstream crate needs.

  Step 5: The codegen backend — rustc_codegen_ssa::traits::CodegenBackend

  make_codegen_backend returns a Box<dyn CodegenBackend>. Rustc invokes its methods in a fixed order:

  1. init(&Session) — one-shot init.
  2. provide(&mut Providers) — the backend can install its own query providers (separate from yours in step 4).
  3. codegen_crate(tcx, metadata, need_metadata_module) -> Box<dyn Any> — walks the monomorphized items and produces CGUs of machine code. Returns an opaque handle.
  4. join_codegen(ongoing, sess, outputs) -> (CodegenResults, WorkProducts) — finalizes all codegen (blocks on any background threads), returns CompiledModules + incremental work
  products.
  5. link(sess, codegen_results, outputs) — invoke the linker.

  The useful pattern is wrap-and-delegate: your backend holds Box<dyn CodegenBackend> pointing at rustc's real LLVMbackend, forwards every method, and modifies inputs or outputs
  at whichever point you care about.

  Two intervention points are especially handy:
  - In join_codegen, append extra pre-compiled objects to codegen_results.modules as additional CompiledModule entries (with ModuleKind::Regular and object: Some(path)). Rustc's
  linker picks them up alongside Rust-compiled modules.
  - Around codegen_crate, run your own backend before or after rustc's.

  rustc_codegen_llvm::LlvmCodegenBackend::new() is callable directly to construct the wrapped inner backend.

  Step 6: Cargo-level interception — RUSTC_WORKSPACE_WRAPPER

  Not a rustc extension point strictly speaking, but relevant. If you set RUSTC_WORKSPACE_WRAPPER=/path/to/your-bin, cargo invokes your binary instead of rustc for primary
  workspace crates, passing the real rustc path as argv[1] and the rustc args as argv[2..]. Non-workspace dependencies get invoked with vanilla rustc, untouched.

  Useful environment variables cargo sets on the wrapped invocation:
  - CARGO_PRIMARY_PACKAGE — set to "1" only for primary-workspace compiles
  - CARGO_MANIFEST_DIR — the per-package source dir
  - CARGO_PKG_NAME — the cargo package name
  - CARGO_TARGET_DIR — the build output root (controllable from outside to share caches)

  The wrapping binary typically inspects argv[1]'s basename for rustc, drops it, detects primary-vs-non-primary viathe env var, and either delegates to plain rustc or dispatches
  into RunCompiler::new with the custom callbacks.

  Step 7: The universal handle — TyCtxt<'tcx>

  Every extension point either receives a TyCtxt<'tcx> directly or can get one through the query it's plugged into.TyCtxt is the central read-side handle:

  - tcx.item_name(def_id), tcx.opt_item_name(def_id) — item names
  - tcx.crate_name(crate_num) — crate name for a CrateNum
  - tcx.def_kind(def_id) — the DefKind (Fn, Struct, Trait, ...)
  - tcx.def_path(def_id) — structural DefPath; safe from any phase
  - tcx.def_path_str(def_id) — human-readable path; only safe during diagnostics, ICEs elsewhere
  - tcx.type_of(def_id) — the item's type (as EarlyBinder<Ty<'tcx>>)
  - tcx.fn_sig(def_id), tcx.fn_abi_of_instance(...) — signature and ABI
  - tcx.layout_of(PseudoCanonicalInput { value, typing_env }) — type layout
  - tcx.module_children_local(def_id) / module_children(...) — enumerate pub use re-exports and module contents, including across crate boundaries
  - tcx.hir_crate_items(()).definitions() / .foreign_items() — walk the local crate
  - tcx.arena — the arena tied to 'tcx. Allocate through it to satisfy lifetime constraints on query returns.

  Step 8: The threading contract

  Queries fire on rayon worker threads. The lifecycle hooks (config, after_analysis) and the CodegenBackend methodsrun on whatever thread rustc's driver is on. Query providers
  fire concurrently, so any state shared across them needs proper synchronization.

  OnceLock is the idiomatic choice for install-once immutable state (saved default providers, config). Mutex for anything mutable. Watch out for re-entrant locking — a query
  provider firing during a phase where your code already holds the lock will silently deadlock (0% CPU, hangs forever).

  Step 9: The nightly pin

  Everything above is #![feature(rustc_private)] territory — no SemVer, APIs drift per nightly. Pin the toolchain (rust-toolchain.toml with a specific channel =
  "nightly-YYYY-MM-DD") so drift is a conscious event. Churn is uneven: rustc_interface::Config, rustc_driver::Callbacks, and the existence of the Providers struct are very
  stable; individual query signatures (especially MIR construction primitives and ABI helpers) drift more often. Budget ~1 week of adaptation per 6-month nightly bump.

❯ heres a report from my researcher, should we think about any of these things? feel free to web search to doublecheck anything.

  # Citations, API drift, and explanatory additions for the rustc extension-points guide

  Below is integration-ready material, organized into three parts exactly as requested:

  1. **Citation suggestions** keyed to spots in the document (section + quote/phrase).
  2. **What's changed since early 2025** — API drift notes with PR numbers.
  3. **Small explanatory additions** (light-to-medium touch), each 1–2 sentences.

  Headline finding on drift: **`RunCompiler` has been removed** (PR #135880, early 2025). The snippet `RunCompiler::new(rustc_args, &mut my_callbacks).run()` no longer compiles
  on recent nightlies — the current entry point is the free function `rustc_driver::run_compiler(&rustc_args, &mut my_callbacks)`. Two other material changes are the `ParamEnv →
  TypingEnv` refactor (PR #132460) and the `CodegenBackend::codegen_crate` / `link` signature change that moved `EncodedMetadata` from `codegen_crate` to `link` (PR #141769).
  Everything else the document describes is still accurate as of April 2026.

  ---

  ## Part 1 — Citation suggestions keyed to spots in the document

  Format: **section** → *quote/phrase* → URL(s) + one-line rationale.

  ### Step 0: The gate — `#![feature(rustc_private)]`

  - *"All of rustc's internal crates … are nightly-only and gated by this feature"* → https://doc.rust-lang.org/unstable-book/language-features/rustc-private.html (the canonical
  unstable-book page for the feature).
  - *"There's no SemVer; APIs drift across nightlies"* → https://rustc-dev-guide.rust-lang.org/rustc-driver/intro.html (dev-guide explicitly warns: "By its very nature, the
  internal compiler APIs are always going to be unstable").
  - *"You also need to link against the compiler's shared libraries at runtime"* → https://jyn.dev/rustc-driver/ (the standard community write-up on the runtime linking / sysroot
   dance; covers macOS/Linux specifically).

  ### Step 1: The driver entry point — `rustc_driver::RunCompiler`

  - *"`rustc_driver::RunCompiler::new(rustc_args, &mut my_callbacks).run();`"* → **must be updated**. Current API (April 2026):
  https://doc.rust-lang.org/nightly/nightly-rustc/rustc_driver/fn.run_compiler.html — see also PR https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/pull/135880 ("Get rid of RunCompiler"). Use
  `rustc_driver::run_compiler(&rustc_args, &mut my_callbacks)`.
  - *"rustc_args is a &[String] in rustc argv shape"* → https://github.com/rus
  ──── (251 lines hidden) ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  s only applies to the local crate" from a runtime panic into a compile-time guarantee."**
  Source: https://doc.rust-lang.org/nightly/nightly-rustc/rustc_span/def_id/struct.LocalDefId.html

  ### A13. Reference drivers (insert at the end of Step 1 or in a "further reading" callout)

  **"The canonical worked examples of `rustc_driver` consumers are `clippy_driver` (overrides lints via `Callbacks::config`) and `miri` (overrides `optimized_mir` and the codegen
   step); both live in `src/tools/` of the `rust-lang/rust` tree. The dev-guide also maintains a minimal example at `rustc-dev-guide/examples/rustc-driver-example.rs`."**
  Source: https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/tree/master/src/tools and https://github.com/rust-lang/rustc-dev-guide/blob/main/examples/rustc-driver-example.rs

  ### A14. Overridden queries still red-green (optional — merge with A2 if tight)

  **"An overridden provider participates in red-green tracking normally: its result is hashed and its dep-edges are recorded from whatever `tcx.*` calls it makes. If you need to
  force re-execution every session (e.g. because your provider depends on external state), declare the query as `eval_always` — but doing so for a hot query like `optimized_mir`
  will quietly wreck compile times."**
  Source: https://rustc-dev-guide.rust-lang.org/queries/incremental-compilation-in-detail.html

  ### A15. On the `BackendRepr` rename (inline parenthetical in 4c)

  **"(`BackendRepr` was called `Abi` in `rustc_target` before PR #132385; old drivers may still see that name in older nightlies.)"**
  Source: https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/pull/132385

  ---

  ## Summary

  Four cross-cutting things to update before merging citations:

  1. **Replace the `RunCompiler::new(...).run()` snippet** with `rustc_driver::run_compiler(&rustc_args, &mut my_callbacks)` and note that `file_loader`/`make_codegen_backend`
  now live on `Config`.
  2. **Update the `CodegenBackend::codegen_crate` signature** to drop `metadata` and `need_metadata_module`, and add the `EncodedMetadata` parameter to `link`.
  3. **Reword the `Reveal::All` parenthetical** in 4a to speak in `TypingMode` terms.
  4. **Pick 8–12 of the A-additions** that match the document's voice; the highest-leverage ones for a tool-builder are A1 (key types), A2 (incremental), A3 (fn-not-Fn), A4
  (sysroot), A6 (MIR validator), A10 (`'tcx` leaks), A12 (DefId vs LocalDefId), and A13 (clippy/miri as references).

  Everything else the document says about rustc extension points is still accurate in April 2026.

⏺ Verified the PR dates against our pin (nightly-2025-01-15):

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────┬───────────┬────────────┬───────────────────────────┐
  │                  PR                   │   What    │   Merged   │    Relative to our pin    │
  ├───────────────────────────────────────┼───────────┼────────────┼───────────────────────────┤
  │ #132385 — Abi → BackendRepr           │ rename    │ 2024-11-01 │ before — guide is correct │
  ├───────────────────────────────────────┼───────────┼────────────┼───────────────────────────┤
  │ #132460 — ParamEnv → TypingEnv        │ migration │ 2024-11-19 │ before — guide is correct │
  ├───────────────────────────────────────┼───────────┼────────────┼───────────────────────────┤
  │ #135880 — RunCompiler removal         │ drift     │ 2025-01-24 │ 9 days after our pin      │
  ├───────────────────────────────────────┼───────────┼────────────┼───────────────────────────┤
  │ #141769 — codegen_crate metadata move │ drift     │ 2025-06-16 │ after                     │
  └───────────────────────────────────────┴───────────┴────────────┴───────────────────────────┘

  So our codebase uses RunCompiler::new(...).run() and the old codegen_crate(tcx, metadata, need_metadata_module) signature — both correct for our toolchain, both obsolete on a
  modern nightly.

  Recommendation

  Drift items: don't rewrite — our code is accurate for our pin and rewriting would actually make it wrong for what's in driver.rs / codegen_wrapper.rs. But since the guide's goal
   is "learn about rustc extension points," add one-sentence footnotes noting the post-pin API so the reader isn't surprised if they go to current docs. Two small notes in §1 and
  §5.

  A-additions — worth adding:
  - A1 (key types — LocalDefId vs Instance vs PseudoCanonicalInput)
  - A2 (queries + incremental / fingerprint determinism)
  - A3 (fn not Fn — non-capturing closure constraint)
  - A4 (sysroot discovery via rustc --print sysroot)
  - A6 (MIR validator / -Zvalidate-mir)
  - A10 ('tcx lifetime escape)
  - A12 (DefId vs LocalDefId)
  - A13 (clippy/miri as reference drivers)

  A-additions — skip or inline:
  - A5, A7, A8, A9, A11, A14, A15 are either tangential, niche, or subsumed by the picks above. A15 (BackendRepr rename) is not useful for our pin since we never saw Abi.

  Citations: mostly redundant for a message-format guide. Useful if we commit the guide as a doc file.

  What I'd do next

  Three options, pick one:

  1. Save as docs/background/rustc-extension-points.md with the drift footnotes + 8 additions + a handful of the most useful citations (rustdoc + dev-guide links only, not every
  one). This makes it a durable reference.
  2. Re-send the guide inline with the drift footnotes + 8 additions, no citations.
  3. Leave as-is; the researcher's report is good context to keep around but doesn't change your pin's reality.

  Which?

  Sources:
  - PR #135880 — Get rid of RunCompiler
  - PR #141769 — Move metadata generation to linker code
  - PR #132460 — TypingMode throughout
  - PR #132385 — ExternAbi rename

⏺ Yes, worth doing, but scope it as dedicated work — not mixed with anything else.

  Where the pin lives

  Anchored in rust-toolchain.toml (canonical) + duplicated at 22 other sites — mix of code (test harnesses, build.rs template, main.rs), docs, and narrative. Bumping the canonical
   file is easy; the cascading updates are the real work.

  What bumping actually buys / costs

  Buys:
  - Smaller gap now vs a huge one later — drift compounds
  - Zero-fork architecture means bump is just "adapt to API drift" — no patch re-port like pre-stage-4
  - Project is in maintenance mode → no feature pressure competing with migration

  Costs (per risks.md B3 budget):
  - ~1 week per 6-month nightly. 15 months ≈ 2–3 weeks realistic
  - Known drift to adapt: RunCompiler removal, codegen_crate signature change, Callbacks new default methods
  - Unknown unknowns: MIR construction (B3), ABI helpers (B4), partitioner internals (B2) — these are where most ofthe week goes

  Target nightly

  Per HANDOFF-TL.md §5: "Don't chase latest nightly. Bump to a ~3-month-old nightly." → target around nightly-2026-01-15. Skips the bleeding edge, captures most of the accumulated
   stability.

  Honest prioritization

  Per the handoff, open threads are: (3a) Vale response, (3b) upstream PR opportunity, (3c) three minor tech-debt items. A nightly bump isn't on that list — the project is
  explicitly at its resting point. The bump is pure hygiene.

  If you're doing hygiene passes, this is one. If you have limited time, Vale response (user-visible) or upstream PR (compounding value) probably rank higher.

  Suggested plan if you do bump

  1. Pre-flight (~1 hour): I do a scoping pass — inventory every pin occurrence, classify each as structural code change vs narrative doc update, flag known API drift sites by
  file+line. Output a punch list, no edits yet.
  2. Execute (dedicated session, ~1-2 weeks): bump rust-toolchain.toml, cargo check, fix each compile error in order, run test suite, fix runtime failures. Don't bundle with other
   work.
  3. Doc sweep: update the 13 doc files that reference the old pin string, update HANDOFF-TL.md test count if it changed, update the nightly bump note in risks.md with the
  empirical cost.

  Want me to do step 1 (scoping pass → punch list, no edits)? That'd make the go/no-go decision concrete without committing you to anything.

⏺ Nightly Bump Scoping — Punch List

  Target: nightly-2026-01-15 (3 months old per HANDOFF-TL.md §5 "don't chase latest")

  ---
  Section 1: Pin occurrences (33 total across 23 files)

  1a. Canonical anchor (1 file, 1 line) — change first

  ┌─────────────────────┬──────┬────────────────────────────────┐
  │        File         │ Line │              What              │
  ├─────────────────────┼──────┼────────────────────────────────┤
  │ rust-toolchain.toml │ 2    │ channel = "nightly-2025-01-15" │
  └─────────────────────┴──────┴────────────────────────────────┘

  This is the single source of truth for bare cargo invocations. Changing this one line + running cargo check is step 1 of the whole bump.

  1b. Hardcoded toolchain strings in code (4 files, 7 sites) — must update together with 1a

  These spawn rustc/cargo with +nightly-2025-01-15 explicitly; ignoring any of them leaves a dangling reference to a toolchain you might have uninstalled.

  Section 2: Known API drift (confirmed by PR dates)

  2a. PR #135880 — RunCompiler removed (merged 2025-01-24)

  Two call sites will fail to compile:

  ┌────────────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │             File:Line              │                            Current                             │            Becomes                        │
  ├────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ rustc-lang-facade/src/driver.rs:52 │ rustc_driver::RunCompiler::new(rustc_args, &mut driver).run(); │ rustc_driver::run_compiler(rustc_args, &mut driver); │
  ├────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ toylangc/src/main.rs:194           │ rustc_driver::RunCompiler::new(args, &mut cb).run();           │ rustc_driver::run_compiler(args, &mut cb);           │
  └────────────────────────────────────┴────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Cost: ~15 min. Trivial signature change, no logic rework.

  2b. PR #141769 — codegen_crate signature narrowed (merged 2025-06-16)

  One trait impl must change shape:

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┬───────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                       File                       │      Method       │                                     Change                                                │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ rustc-lang-facade/src/codegen_wrapper.rs:72-77   │ codegen_crate     │ Drop metadata: EncodedMetadata and need_metadata_module: bool params                                │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ rustc-lang-facade/src/codegen_wrapper.rs:99      │ codegen_crate     │ self.inner.codegen_crate(tcx, metadata, need_metadata_module) → self.inner.codegen_crate(tcx)       │
  │                                                  │ body              │                                                                                                     │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ rustc-lang-facade/src/codegen_wrapper.rs:141-148 │ link              │ Add metadata: EncodedMetadata parameter, forward to self.inner.link(sess, codegen_results,          │
  │                                                  │                   │ metadata, outputs)                                                           │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────┴───────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Cost: ~30 min. Trivial once signatures are known; the forwarding pattern preserves all existing logic.

  2c. Callbacks trait additions (non-breaking on current nightly)

  after_crate_root_parsing and after_expansion were added to rustc_driver::Callbacks with default impls. Our impls at driver.rs:57 and main.rs:188 only override config /
  after_analysis — no code change required.

  Cost: 0 min. Mentioned for completeness.

  2d. ParamEnv → TypingEnv (merged 2024-11-19, predates our pin)

  Our codebase already uses TypingEnv::fully_monomorphized(). No change.

  2e. Abi → BackendRepr (merged 2024-11-01, predates our pin)

  Our codebase already uses BackendRepr. No change.

  ---
  Section 3: Unknown-drift surfaces (expect 1–3 weeks of repair)

  Per risks.md, these are the dominant cost centers. Exact drift unknown until the new nightly is checked; order ofprobability:

  3a. MIR construction (risks.md B3 — "~100% per bump")

  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                      File                      │         Function         │         Risk                                              │
  ├────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │                                                │ build_dependency_body    │ High. Constructs Statement, Terminator, Rvalue::Cast,                                          │
  │ rustc-lang-facade/src/queries/optimized_mir.rs │ (~110 LoC)               │ CastKind::PointerCoercion(ReifyFnPointer, CoercionSource), NullOp::SizeOf, SourceInfo,         │
  │                                                │                          │ ClearCrossCrate. Every one of these has drifted in prior bumps.                                │
  ├────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ rustc-lang-facade/src/mir_helpers.rs           │ build_drop_call_body     │ High. TerminatorKind::Call, Spanned, UnwindAction, CallSource, Body::new. Same drift surface.  │
  │                                                │ (~100 LoC)               │                                                                                                │
  ├────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ rustc-lang-facade/src/queries/drop_glue.rs     │ dispatch logic           │ Low. Pattern-matches InstanceKind::DropGlue — enum variant name has been stable.               │
  └────────────────────────────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────┴────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Cost: 3–7 days realistically. Repair loop: cargo check → adapt a signature → repeat. -Zvalidate-mir is your friend here; budget time for it catching things the compile step
  misses.

  3b. ABI helpers (risks.md B4 — "15–25% per 5yr, but accumulates")

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                 File                 │                         Surface                          │                    Risk                                   │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ rustc-lang-facade/src/abi_helpers.rs │ CoercedReturn, CoercedParam, cast_target_to_llvm_str,    │ Medium. Pattern-matches PassMode, BackendRepr, rustc_abi::Primitive,     │
  │                                      │ primitive_to_llvm_str, reg_to_llvm_str                   │ Reg, RegKind,CastTarget. PassMode variants have split before.           │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ toylangc/src/llvm_gen.rs             │ Consumers of the above at every Rust-method call site    │ Low-medium. If abi_helpers.rs repairs cleanly, llvm_gen follows. If they │
  │                                      │                                                          │  introduce new variants we don't handle, more work.                      │
  └──────────────────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Cost: 2–5 days. Usually localized — a single PassMode variant change is a handful of match arms.

  3c. Partitioner / CGU (risks.md B2, B5)

  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                    File                    │                Surface                │              Risk                                          │
  ├────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │                                            │                                       │ Medium. Relies on MonoItemData's public shape + the "plugin's post-partition mutation  │
  │ rustc-lang-facade/src/queries/partition.rs │ lang_collect_and_partition_mono_items │ survives to LLVM emission"timing assumption. If internalization phase timing shifts,  │
  │                                            │                                       │ Phase-6 unwrap tests fail with link errors.                                            │
  ├────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ rustc-lang-facade/src/cgu_stash.rs         │ CodegenUnit<'tcx> pointer stashing    │ Medium. Raw-pointer + lifetime-erasure trick. If rustc changes CodegenUnit's arena     │
  │                                            │                                       │ semantics, the stash needs redesign.                                                   │
  └────────────────────────────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────────────┴────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Cost: Best case 0 days (stays working); worst case 1–2 weeks (retreat to fork hook per B2 §Reaction). First tests to fail: Phase-6 test_option_unwrap_basic,
  test_result_unwrap_basic, test_unwrap_arithmetic_chain.

  3d. Query provider signatures (risks.md C3)

  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                            Surface                                             │                   Risk                                    │
  ├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ layout_of, symbol_name, optimized_mir, mir_shims, collect_and_partition_mono_items,            │ Low. These arethe most stable part of the extension surface. Key/return  │
  │ upstream_monomorphizations_for typedefs in queries/*.rs                                        │ shape has beenstable for multiple nightlies.                             │
  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  LayoutOfFn in queries/layout.rs:34-37 is the most likely to drift — its comment explicitly warns that the input type has shifted before.

  ---
  Section 4: Proposed execution order

  Once you commit to bumping, execute in this order (don't interleave):

  1. Snapshot: note current test count (210), run cargo check → clean baseline.
  2. Update canonical pin: edit rust-toolchain.toml:2. Install the new toolchain. Don't update code-level strings yet.
  3. Install rust-src and rustc-dev: rustup component add --toolchain nightly-2026-01-15 rustc-dev rust-src llvm-tools-preview.
  4. Compile loop — facade first: cargo check -p rustc-lang-facade. Fix everything under rustc-lang-facade/src/ in order: §2a, §2b, §3a, §3b, §3c. Run until clean.
  5. Compile loop — consumer: cargo check -p toylangc. Fix llvm_gen.rs, callbacks_impl.rs, oracle.rs — whatever broke from facade changes cascading in.
  6. Update hardcoded strings: §1b (7 code sites) — recommend introducing const TOYLANG_NIGHTLY during this bump to make future bumps trivial.
  7. Unit tests: cargo test -p toylangc --bins. Target: 67/67.
  8. Integration tests: cargo test -p toylangc --test integration_projects. Target: 128/128. If Phase-6 unwrap tests fail, you hit §3c — escalate per risks.md B2.
  9. Standalone tests: cargo test -p toylangc --test standalone_tests. Target: 15/15.
  10. Doc sweep: §1c (21 sites). Update risks.md §280 with the empirical cost of this bump so the next maintainer has real data.
  11. HANDOFF-TL.md: bump the pin reference in §1, §4, §8, §9 sanity check.
  12. Commit as single PR — not spread across work.

  ---
  Section 5: Cost estimate

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬──────────────┐
  │                      Component                      │    Budget    │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────┤
  │ §2a + §2b + §2c + §1a-b (known drift + pin updates) │ ~1 day       │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────┤
  │ §3a MIR construction                                │ 3–7 days     │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────┤
  │ §3b ABI helpers                                     │ 2–5 days     │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────┤
  │ §3c partitioner (best case)                         │ 0–1 day      │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────┤
  │ §3c partitioner (worst case — B2 fires)             │ +1–2 weeks   │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────┤
  │ Tests + doc sweep                                   │ 1–2 days     │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────┤
  │ Total — expected                                    │ ~1.5–2 weeks │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────┤
  │ Total — if B2 fires                                 │ ~3–4 weeks   │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴──────────────┘

  Aligns with risks.md B3 budget ("~1 week per 6-month bump" × 2.5 bumps = 2.5 weeks).

  ---
  Section 6: Pre-commit go/no-go

  Go if:
  - You have 2 dedicated weeks without feature work
  - You're prepared to escalate to a small fork if B2 fires (risks.md §6)
  - The architectural reward (clean slate, no future compound drift) matches your hygiene priority

  Defer if:
  - Vale replies and needs engagement first
  - You'd rather start the upstream LlvmCodegenBackend PR (§3b of HANDOFF)
  - You don't have 2 consecutive weeks of focus

  Your call. Ready to execute if you want, or happy to leave the punch list as a pre-flight artifact for whenever.

⏺ Created at /Users/verdagon/erw/HANDOFF-nightly-bump.md (658 lines).

  Structure

  12 sections, matching the project's junior-handoff house style:

  1. Metadata header — risk level (medium), budget (1.5–2 wk expected, 3–4 wk worst), final state
  2. Context — why, what prompted it, intended outcome
  3. Required reading — 3–4 hours, ordered by priority, risks.md §B2/B3/B4/B5 flagged as most important pre-bump doc
  4. Pin occurrences — exact file:line for all 33 sites, classified into 4 tiers (canonical/code/docs/historical-don't-touch)
  5. Known API drift — the 3 confirmed PR changes with copy-paste-ready before/after diffs at exact file:line
  6. Unknown drift surfaces — the 4 risks.md cost-centers with canary tests and repair strategies
  7. Implementation steps — 16 staged sub-phases, each a commit point with test expectations
  8. Critical subtleties — 10 items covering things that will bite (cache contamination, +pin habits, B6 walk, the DPSFDOZ and GCMLZ arcana)
  9. Verification — exact test-count expectations, the grep that confirms no pin strings remain
  10. Out of scope — 9 things explicitly forbidden (features, refactoring, Vale, fork introduction)
  11. If stuck — escalation gates explicitly list §5.3 (partitioner/B2) as "contact TL immediately, do NOT fork unilaterally"; self-recovery tactics for the non-escalation cases
  12. Rollback + one-paragraph recap

  Escalation gate

  Per your instruction, §5.3 and §10.1 both state the B2 escalation rule explicitly:

  ▎ If §5.3 fires (Phase-6 link errors after the bump): do NOT retreat to a fork on your own initiative. Send the diagnostic packet described in §5.3. This is a project-shape
  ▎ decision, not a junior decision.

  The diagnostic packet is pre-specified (test names, linker output, git log, relevant PR search) so the escalation is actionable the moment it fires.

  Ready to hand to a junior whenever you want.

⏺ Verified — 10 commits clean, well-named per staging convention, unit tests 67/67 on the new toolchain, working tree has exactly the 3 Vale-response files the junior flagged.

  Status review

  Bump quality looks solid. Commit boundaries match the handoff's "arcana-sized change per commit" rule:
  - 636baa8 (Providers restructure) — the biggest unknown-drift find; the junior correctly flagged it in their cover note as "surface not in the handoff." They also added a
  warning in the risks.md doc-sweep commit for the next bump. Good handoff hygiene.
  - 54ae1fd — MIR construction drift (B3) — handled without escalation. NullaryOp removal forced the assert!(is_fn_like) shape in build_dependency_body, which is actually cleaner
  than the pre-bump branch.
  - 78b178b — Layout surface absorbed 4 distinct drift items in one commit (AbiAlign, uninhabited, Hash64, in_memory_order) — reasonable since they're one file and one logical
  "the rustc_abi LayoutData shape shifted" repair.

  B2 held. No partitioner escalation. Phase-6 unwrap tests green.

  Empirical cost data landed in risks.md per §6.13 — the next bump will have real calibration.

  Three loose ends

  1. Uncommitted Vale-response edits (the 3 files)

  These are my edits from earlier in this session (pre-bump) that the junior carefully preserved uncommitted. They're semantically independent from the bump: the Vale response was
   sent 2026-04-20, doc-tracking of that send is its own change. My recommendation: commit them as one focused commit before pushing — leaving them uncommitted indefinitely is
  awkward and the next doc sweep will compound.

  Suggested message: Record Vale response sent 2026-04-20.

  Want me to commit?

  2. Cache-fragility caveat

  The junior flagged this as pre-existing, not a bump regression. Worth filing as a tech-debt entry (#30?) so it doesn't get lost. Symptom is specific: t_of_r_layout /
  t_of_t_layout after running --test X followed by the full suite on a shared target dir. Fix is wiping caches. Theroot cause is probably in the eprintln! emission path in
  layout.rs being incremental-skippable (same class of issue as B6 but different symptom, since the populate walk in generate_and_compile doesn't cover layout-log emission).

  Want me to draft the tech-debt entry?

  3. Final PR

  Handoff §6.16 said "one PR, descriptive title." 10 commits are staged but not pushed. Before pushing I'd:

  - Commit the Vale edits (above)
  - Re-verify full 210/210 on a clean rebuild to rule out cache-fragility false-greens (the junior did this, but the caveat flags that it can flake — worth a fresh run)

⏺ rustc Extension Points — A Step-by-Step Guide

  This walks through the rustc hooks this project touches, in roughly the order they fire. It's scoped to what's actually used here; rustc has many more extension points that
  aren't relevant.

  Step 0: The gate — #![feature(rustc_private)]

  All of rustc's internal crates (rustc_driver, rustc_interface, rustc_middle, rustc_codegen_ssa, rustc_codegen_llvm, rustc_monomorphize, ...) are nightly-only and gated by this
  feature. A crate that depends on them declares it at the crate root. There's no SemVer; APIs drift across nightlies. It's the official "all bets off" escape hatch.

  You also need to link against the compiler's shared libraries at runtime. On macOS that means setting DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH to the sysroot's lib/; on Linux it's LD_LIBRARY_PATH.

  Step 1: The driver entry point — rustc_driver::RunCompiler

  rustc_driver::RunCompiler::new(rustc_args, &mut my_callbacks).run();

  rustc_args is a &[String] in rustc argv shape (skip argv[0]; the rest is whatever the rustc CLI would accept). my_callbacks is any type implementing rustc_driver::Callbacks.
  run() drives the whole compilation.

  Small sibling utilities:
  - rustc_driver::install_ice_hook(report_url, |_| {}) — installs a panic hook that prints a "please report at " onICE.
  - rustc_driver::catch_with_exit_code(|| { ... }) — maps rustc's panic/early-exit patterns into a clean process exit code.

  Step 2: The lifecycle hooks — rustc_driver::Callbacks

  trait Callbacks {
      fn config(&mut self, config: &mut Config) {}
      fn after_analysis<'tcx>(&mut self, compiler: &Compiler, tcx: TyCtxt<'tcx>)
          -> Compilation { Compilation::Continue }
      // a few more, all defaulted
  }

  Two methods matter:

  - config fires very early, before parsing. You mutate the Config struct to install query overrides, swap the codegen backend, register a file loader, etc. This is your only
  chance to hook things in before rustc starts real work.
  - after_analysis fires after type-checking / borrow-checking, before monomorphization. You get a TyCtxt<'tcx> andcan walk the HIR, run validation, emit diagnostics. Return
  Compilation::Continue to proceed or Compilation::Stop to halt.

  Step 3: The config struct — rustc_interface::Config

  Two fields are relevant here:

  config.override_queries    = Some(my_override_fn);
  config.make_codegen_backend = Some(Box::new(|_opts| MyBackend::new()));

  Both are callbacks rustc invokes at the right moment during setup. override_queries is a plain fn pointer: fn(&Session, &mut Providers). make_codegen_backend is a boxed closure
  returning Box<dyn CodegenBackend>.

  Step 4: The query provider table — rustc_middle::util::Providers

  Rustc's compilation is a graph of queries — demand-driven, memoized computations each keyed by some input and returning some output. layout_of, symbol_name, optimized_mir,
  fn_abi_of_instance, and hundreds more. They are the backbone of incremental compilation and lazy evaluation.

  Providers is a big struct of fn pointers, one field per query. Rustc populates it with defaults before calling your override. Your override replaces specific fields:

  fn my_override(_sess: &Session, providers: &mut Providers) {
      // Save the default if you want to delegate to it:
      DEFAULT_OPTIMIZED_MIR.set(providers.optimized_mir).ok();
      providers.optimized_mir = my_provider_fn;
  }

  The idiomatic pattern: stash the default in a OnceLock, have your replacement either handle the input itself or fall through to the saved default for inputs you don't care
  about.

  Below are the six queries this project overrides. Each is its own extension point with a distinct key, return type, and firing semantics.

  4a. optimized_mir

  ┌─────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │         │                                                                  │
  ├─────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Key     │ LocalDefId                                                       │
  ├─────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Returns │ &'tcx Body<'tcx> — the optimized MIR for the function            │
  ├─────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Fires   │ during monomorphization, once per function whose MIR is demanded │
  └─────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Return a hand-built MIR body and rustc's mono collector walks whatever you return. Call terminators queue calleesfor monomorphization.
  Rvalue::Cast(CastKind::PointerCoercion(ReifyFnPointer, _)) queues the target Instance for codegen even if it's never called. NullOp::SizeOf queues a type.

  Constructing valid MIR is fiddly:
  - set_required_consts and set_mentioned_items must be called on synthetic bodies (the normal mir_promoted pass doesn't run for them, so the mono collector panics if they're
  unset).
  - Every BasicBlockData needs a terminator. TerminatorKind::Unreachable is valid for bodies that are never executed.
  - TypingEnv::fully_monomorphized() is a typing-mode flag (PostAnalysis + Reveal::All), not an input assertion — bodies containing ty::TyKind::Param placeholders flow through
  cleanly; rustc's collector substitutes them per caller when walking the body.

  4b. symbol_name

  ┌─────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │         │                                                                                        │
  ├─────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Key     │ Instance<'tcx> — a (DefId, GenericArgsRef<'tcx>) bundle with erased late-bound regions │
  ├─────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Returns │ ty::SymbolName<'tcx>                                                                   │
  ├─────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Fires   │ when rustc needs the linker-visible name for a specific monomorphization               │
  └─────────┴────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Return any string via SymbolName::new(tcx, &s). Useful for redirecting mangled names to your own scheme.

  Important subtlety: computing a symbol name does not drive codegen. It's a pure read. If you only hook symbol_name you'll get "undefined symbol" link errors — driving emission
  requires something like ReifyFnPointer in a MIR body (4a).

  4c. layout_of

  ┌─────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │         │                                                           │
  ├─────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Key     │ PseudoCanonicalInput<'tcx, Ty<'tcx>> (on this nightly; older nightlies used ParamEnvAnd)                                                           │
  ├─────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Returns │ Result<TyAndLayout<'tcx>, &'tcx LayoutError<'tcx>>                                                           │
  ├─────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Fires   │ extremely high volume — once for every type whose size/alignment rustc needs, including derived forms(*mut T, &T, Option<T>, tuples containing your type, etc.) │
  └─────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Return a LayoutData describing the type's memory shape. BackendRepr::Memory { sized: true } tells rustc it's an opaque blob — no scalar-pair decomposition, no niche
  optimization.

  Filter aggressively. You want to intercept TyKind::Adt only for specific types; intercepting derived forms corrupts their layouts and ICEs codegen. Check has_param() on the args
   to skip generic definitions that haven't been instantiated yet.

  4d. mir_shims

  ┌─────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │         │      │
  ├─────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Key     │ ty::InstanceKind<'tcx> — variants include DropGlue(DefId, Option<Ty<'tcx>>), FnPtrShim(...), CloneShim(...) │
  ├─────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Returns │ Body<'tcx>      │
  ├─────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Fires   │ when rustc needs a generated shim body      │
  └─────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Pattern-match the InstanceKind and synthesize a MIR body. DropGlue(_, Some(ty)) is the common target — you can return a body that calls a custom destructor.

  4e. collect_and_partition_mono_items

  ┌─────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │         │                                                                                       │
  ├─────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Key     │ () (singleton query)                                                                  │
  ├─────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Returns │ (&'tcx DefIdSet, &'tcx [CodegenUnit<'tcx>]) — the reachable-items set + CGU partition │
  ├─────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Fires   │ once per compilation, the bridge between mono collection and codegen                  │
  └─────────┴───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Delegate to the upstream provider, then reconstruct the returned CGU slice — filter items out, or mutate MonoItemData fields (linkage, visibility) on items that remain. The LLVM
   backend reads data.linkage directly from the CGU slice it receives; it doesn't re-query mono_item_linkage_and_visibility, so post-partition mutations survive to LLVM emission.

  Useful for removing items from rustc's codegen path or forcing specific linkage decisions without a source-level #[linkage] attribute.

  4f. upstream_monomorphizations_for

  ┌─────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │         │                                     │
  ├─────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Key     │ LocalDefId                                     │
  ├─────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Returns │ Option<&'tcx UnordMap<GenericArgsRef<'tcx>, CrateNum>>                                     │
  ├─────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Fires   │ during mono collection when deciding whether a generic monomorphization should be linked from an upstream rlib rather than emitted locally │
  └─────────┴────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Return None to force local emission. Matters when generic items live in an rlib that doesn't contain the specificinstantiation a downstream crate needs.

  Step 5: The codegen backend — rustc_codegen_ssa::traits::CodegenBackend

  make_codegen_backend returns a Box<dyn CodegenBackend>. Rustc invokes its methods in a fixed order:

  1. init(&Session) — one-shot init.
  2. provide(&mut Providers) — the backend can install its own query providers (separate from yours in step 4).
  3. codegen_crate(tcx, metadata, need_metadata_module) -> Box<dyn Any> — walks the monomorphized items and produces CGUs of machine code. Returns an opaque handle.
  4. join_codegen(ongoing, sess, outputs) -> (CodegenResults, WorkProducts) — finalizes all codegen (blocks on any background threads), returns CompiledModules + incremental work
  products.
  5. link(sess, codegen_results, outputs) — invoke the linker.

  The useful pattern is wrap-and-delegate: your backend holds Box<dyn CodegenBackend> pointing at rustc's real LLVMbackend, forwards every method, and modifies inputs or outputs
  at whichever point you care about.

  Two intervention points are especially handy:
  - In join_codegen, append extra pre-compiled objects to codegen_results.modules as additional CompiledModule entries (with ModuleKind::Regular and object: Some(path)). Rustc's
  linker picks them up alongside Rust-compiled modules.
  - Around codegen_crate, run your own backend before or after rustc's.

  rustc_codegen_llvm::LlvmCodegenBackend::new() is callable directly to construct the wrapped inner backend.

  Step 6: Cargo-level interception — RUSTC_WORKSPACE_WRAPPER

  Not a rustc extension point strictly speaking, but relevant. If you set RUSTC_WORKSPACE_WRAPPER=/path/to/your-bin, cargo invokes your binary instead of rustc for primary
  workspace crates, passing the real rustc path as argv[1] and the rustc args as argv[2..]. Non-workspace dependencies get invoked with vanilla rustc, untouched.

  Useful environment variables cargo sets on the wrapped invocation:
  - CARGO_PRIMARY_PACKAGE — set to "1" only for primary-workspace compiles
  - CARGO_MANIFEST_DIR — the per-package source dir
  - CARGO_PKG_NAME — the cargo package name
  - CARGO_TARGET_DIR — the build output root (controllable from outside to share caches)

  The wrapping binary typically inspects argv[1]'s basename for rustc, drops it, detects primary-vs-non-primary viathe env var, and either delegates to plain rustc or dispatches
  into RunCompiler::new with the custom callbacks.

  Step 7: The universal handle — TyCtxt<'tcx>

  Every extension point either receives a TyCtxt<'tcx> directly or can get one through the query it's plugged into.TyCtxt is the central read-side handle:

  - tcx.item_name(def_id), tcx.opt_item_name(def_id) — item names
  - tcx.crate_name(crate_num) — crate name for a CrateNum
  - tcx.def_kind(def_id) — the DefKind (Fn, Struct, Trait, ...)
  - tcx.def_path(def_id) — structural DefPath; safe from any phase
  - tcx.def_path_str(def_id) — human-readable path; only safe during diagnostics, ICEs elsewhere
  - tcx.type_of(def_id) — the item's type (as EarlyBinder<Ty<'tcx>>)
  - tcx.fn_sig(def_id), tcx.fn_abi_of_instance(...) — signature and ABI
  - tcx.layout_of(PseudoCanonicalInput { value, typing_env }) — type layout
  - tcx.module_children_local(def_id) / module_children(...) — enumerate pub use re-exports and module contents, including across crate boundaries
  - tcx.hir_crate_items(()).definitions() / .foreign_items() — walk the local crate
  - tcx.arena — the arena tied to 'tcx. Allocate through it to satisfy lifetime constraints on query returns.

  Step 8: The threading contract

  Queries fire on rayon worker threads. The lifecycle hooks (config, after_analysis) and the CodegenBackend methodsrun on whatever thread rustc's driver is on. Query providers
  fire concurrently, so any state shared across them needs proper synchronization.

  OnceLock is the idiomatic choice for install-once immutable state (saved default providers, config). Mutex for anything mutable. Watch out for re-entrant locking — a query
  provider firing during a phase where your code already holds the lock will silently deadlock (0% CPU, hangs forever).

  Step 9: The nightly pin

  Everything above is #![feature(rustc_private)] territory — no SemVer, APIs drift per nightly. Pin the toolchain (rust-toolchain.toml with a specific channel =
  "nightly-YYYY-MM-DD") so drift is a conscious event. Churn is uneven: rustc_interface::Config, rustc_driver::Callbacks, and the existence of the Providers struct are very
  stable; individual query signatures (especially MIR construction primitives and ABI helpers) drift more often. Budget ~1 week of adaptation per 6-month nightly bump.

❯ heres a report from my researcher, should we think about any of these things? feel free to web search to doublecheck anything.

  # Citations, API drift, and explanatory additions for the rustc extension-points guide

  Below is integration-ready material, organized into three parts exactly as requested:

  1. **Citation suggestions** keyed to spots in the document (section + quote/phrase).
  2. **What's changed since early 2025** — API drift notes with PR numbers.
  3. **Small explanatory additions** (light-to-medium touch), each 1–2 sentences.

  Headline finding on drift: **`RunCompiler` has been removed** (PR #135880, early 2025). The snippet `RunCompiler::new(rustc_args, &mut my_callbacks).run()` no longer compiles
  on recent nightlies — the current entry point is the free function `rustc_driver::run_compiler(&rustc_args, &mut my_callbacks)`. Two other material changes are the `ParamEnv →
  TypingEnv` refactor (PR #132460) and the `CodegenBackend::codegen_crate` / `link` signature change that moved `EncodedMetadata` from `codegen_crate` to `link` (PR #141769).
  Everything else the document describes is still accurate as of April 2026.

  ---

  ## Part 1 — Citation suggestions keyed to spots in the document

  Format: **section** → *quote/phrase* → URL(s) + one-line rationale.

  ### Step 0: The gate — `#![feature(rustc_private)]`

  - *"All of rustc's internal crates … are nightly-only and gated by this feature"* → https://doc.rust-lang.org/unstable-book/language-features/rustc-private.html (the canonical
  unstable-book page for the feature).
  - *"There's no SemVer; APIs drift across nightlies"* → https://rustc-dev-guide.rust-lang.org/rustc-driver/intro.html (dev-guide explicitly warns: "By its very nature, the
  internal compiler APIs are always going to be unstable").
  - *"You also need to link against the compiler's shared libraries at runtime"* → https://jyn.dev/rustc-driver/ (the standard community write-up on the runtime linking / sysroot
   dance; covers macOS/Linux specifically).

  ### Step 1: The driver entry point — `rustc_driver::RunCompiler`

  - *"`rustc_driver::RunCompiler::new(rustc_args, &mut my_callbacks).run();`"* → **must be updated**. Current API (April 2026):
  https://doc.rust-lang.org/nightly/nightly-rustc/rustc_driver/fn.run_compiler.html — see also PR https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/pull/135880 ("Get rid of RunCompiler"). Use
  `rustc_driver::run_compiler(&rustc_args, &mut my_callbacks)`.
  - *"rustc_args is a &[String] in rustc argv shape"* → https://github.com/rus
  ──── (251 lines hidden) ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  s only applies to the local crate" from a runtime panic into a compile-time guarantee."**
  Source: https://doc.rust-lang.org/nightly/nightly-rustc/rustc_span/def_id/struct.LocalDefId.html

  ### A13. Reference drivers (insert at the end of Step 1 or in a "further reading" callout)

  **"The canonical worked examples of `rustc_driver` consumers are `clippy_driver` (overrides lints via `Callbacks::config`) and `miri` (overrides `optimized_mir` and the codegen
   step); both live in `src/tools/` of the `rust-lang/rust` tree. The dev-guide also maintains a minimal example at `rustc-dev-guide/examples/rustc-driver-example.rs`."**
  Source: https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/tree/master/src/tools and https://github.com/rust-lang/rustc-dev-guide/blob/main/examples/rustc-driver-example.rs

  ### A14. Overridden queries still red-green (optional — merge with A2 if tight)

  **"An overridden provider participates in red-green tracking normally: its result is hashed and its dep-edges are recorded from whatever `tcx.*` calls it makes. If you need to
  force re-execution every session (e.g. because your provider depends on external state), declare the query as `eval_always` — but doing so for a hot query like `optimized_mir`
  will quietly wreck compile times."**
  Source: https://rustc-dev-guide.rust-lang.org/queries/incremental-compilation-in-detail.html

  ### A15. On the `BackendRepr` rename (inline parenthetical in 4c)

  **"(`BackendRepr` was called `Abi` in `rustc_target` before PR #132385; old drivers may still see that name in older nightlies.)"**
  Source: https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/pull/132385

  ---

  ## Summary

  Four cross-cutting things to update before merging citations:

  1. **Replace the `RunCompiler::new(...).run()` snippet** with `rustc_driver::run_compiler(&rustc_args, &mut my_callbacks)` and note that `file_loader`/`make_codegen_backend`
  now live on `Config`.
  2. **Update the `CodegenBackend::codegen_crate` signature** to drop `metadata` and `need_metadata_module`, and add the `EncodedMetadata` parameter to `link`.
  3. **Reword the `Reveal::All` parenthetical** in 4a to speak in `TypingMode` terms.
  4. **Pick 8–12 of the A-additions** that match the document's voice; the highest-leverage ones for a tool-builder are A1 (key types), A2 (incremental), A3 (fn-not-Fn), A4
  (sysroot), A6 (MIR validator), A10 (`'tcx` leaks), A12 (DefId vs LocalDefId), and A13 (clippy/miri as references).

  Everything else the document says about rustc extension points is still accurate in April 2026.

⏺ Verified the PR dates against our pin (nightly-2025-01-15):

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────┬───────────┬────────────┬───────────────────────────┐
  │                  PR                   │   What    │   Merged   │    Relative to our pin    │
  ├───────────────────────────────────────┼───────────┼────────────┼───────────────────────────┤
  │ #132385 — Abi → BackendRepr           │ rename    │ 2024-11-01 │ before — guide is correct │
  ├───────────────────────────────────────┼───────────┼────────────┼───────────────────────────┤
  │ #132460 — ParamEnv → TypingEnv        │ migration │ 2024-11-19 │ before — guide is correct │
  ├───────────────────────────────────────┼───────────┼────────────┼───────────────────────────┤
  │ #135880 — RunCompiler removal         │ drift     │ 2025-01-24 │ 9 days after our pin      │
  ├───────────────────────────────────────┼───────────┼────────────┼───────────────────────────┤
  │ #141769 — codegen_crate metadata move │ drift     │ 2025-06-16 │ after                     │
  └───────────────────────────────────────┴───────────┴────────────┴───────────────────────────┘

  So our codebase uses RunCompiler::new(...).run() and the old codegen_crate(tcx, metadata, need_metadata_module) signature — both correct for our toolchain, both obsolete on a
  modern nightly.

  Recommendation

  Drift items: don't rewrite — our code is accurate for our pin and rewriting would actually make it wrong for what's in driver.rs / codegen_wrapper.rs. But since the guide's goal
   is "learn about rustc extension points," add one-sentence footnotes noting the post-pin API so the reader isn't surprised if they go to current docs. Two small notes in §1 and
  §5.

  A-additions — worth adding:
  - A1 (key types — LocalDefId vs Instance vs PseudoCanonicalInput)
  - A2 (queries + incremental / fingerprint determinism)
  - A3 (fn not Fn — non-capturing closure constraint)
  - A4 (sysroot discovery via rustc --print sysroot)
  - A6 (MIR validator / -Zvalidate-mir)
  - A10 ('tcx lifetime escape)
  - A12 (DefId vs LocalDefId)
  - A13 (clippy/miri as reference drivers)

  A-additions — skip or inline:
  - A5, A7, A8, A9, A11, A14, A15 are either tangential, niche, or subsumed by the picks above. A15 (BackendRepr rename) is not useful for our pin since we never saw Abi.

  Citations: mostly redundant for a message-format guide. Useful if we commit the guide as a doc file.

  What I'd do next

  Three options, pick one:

  1. Save as docs/background/rustc-extension-points.md with the drift footnotes + 8 additions + a handful of the most useful citations (rustdoc + dev-guide links only, not every
  one). This makes it a durable reference.
  2. Re-send the guide inline with the drift footnotes + 8 additions, no citations.
  3. Leave as-is; the researcher's report is good context to keep around but doesn't change your pin's reality.

  Which?

  Sources:
  - PR #135880 — Get rid of RunCompiler
  - PR #141769 — Move metadata generation to linker code
  - PR #132460 — TypingMode throughout
  - PR #132385 — ExternAbi rename

⏺ Yes, worth doing, but scope it as dedicated work — not mixed with anything else.

  Where the pin lives

  Anchored in rust-toolchain.toml (canonical) + duplicated at 22 other sites — mix of code (test harnesses, build.rs template, main.rs), docs, and narrative. Bumping the canonical
   file is easy; the cascading updates are the real work.

  What bumping actually buys / costs

  Buys:
  - Smaller gap now vs a huge one later — drift compounds
  - Zero-fork architecture means bump is just "adapt to API drift" — no patch re-port like pre-stage-4
  - Project is in maintenance mode → no feature pressure competing with migration

  Costs (per risks.md B3 budget):
  - ~1 week per 6-month nightly. 15 months ≈ 2–3 weeks realistic
  - Known drift to adapt: RunCompiler removal, codegen_crate signature change, Callbacks new default methods
  - Unknown unknowns: MIR construction (B3), ABI helpers (B4), partitioner internals (B2) — these are where most ofthe week goes

  Target nightly

  Per HANDOFF-TL.md §5: "Don't chase latest nightly. Bump to a ~3-month-old nightly." → target around nightly-2026-01-15. Skips the bleeding edge, captures most of the accumulated
   stability.

  Honest prioritization

  Per the handoff, open threads are: (3a) Vale response, (3b) upstream PR opportunity, (3c) three minor tech-debt items. A nightly bump isn't on that list — the project is
  explicitly at its resting point. The bump is pure hygiene.

  If you're doing hygiene passes, this is one. If you have limited time, Vale response (user-visible) or upstream PR (compounding value) probably rank higher.

  Suggested plan if you do bump

  1. Pre-flight (~1 hour): I do a scoping pass — inventory every pin occurrence, classify each as structural code change vs narrative doc update, flag known API drift sites by
  file+line. Output a punch list, no edits yet.
  2. Execute (dedicated session, ~1-2 weeks): bump rust-toolchain.toml, cargo check, fix each compile error in order, run test suite, fix runtime failures. Don't bundle with other
   work.
  3. Doc sweep: update the 13 doc files that reference the old pin string, update HANDOFF-TL.md test count if it changed, update the nightly bump note in risks.md with the
  empirical cost.

  Want me to do step 1 (scoping pass → punch list, no edits)? That'd make the go/no-go decision concrete without committing you to anything.

     511:Stage 5 is the "make erw architecturally Vale-fork-ready" work. The functional gain (Outcome A → source-attribute fast-path for linkage) is real but moderate; the
     strategic gain (architecture-complete for Rust-as-top-level cases + clean fork-ready shape) is larger. Treat the work as "improving the architectural foundation" rather than
     "fixing a problem" — erw works today and will continue to; stage 5 is about what it looks like for someone else to build on.
