❯ qq: part of our stuff's logic is called from rust's mono pass. is that strictly necessary? could we do all that logic up front (just before rust mono), produce a map, and make rust mono's callback just
   lookup in a map?

  separate q: right now it also calls into us to get a symbol name. is there a way facade can generate the names of the things and immediate hand those back to rustc without calling into consumer code,
  and consumer can do its monomorphization separately?

  context: i'd really like to make this entire thing more "library-ish", where rustc calls back into consumer code as little as possible. the more i can think of erw as a library, a tool that the
  consumer controls, the more comfortable id be

⏺ Both are tractable. Short version: yes to Q1 with a caveat, yes to Q2 cleanly.

  Q1: pre-compute + lookup instead of callback during mono

  The architectural shape works. collect_generic_rust_deps is already DefId-keyed (not Instance-keyed), and its output — (DefId, GenericArgsRef) pairs with Params intact — is a pure function of the
  consumer's registry + tcx's type resolution. Nothing about that computation actually requires rustc's mono phase to be running. You could:

  1. In after_analysis (before mono starts), walk the consumer registry and populate HashMap<LocalDefId, Vec<(DefId, GenericArgsRef<'tcx>)>>.
  2. The optimized_mir override becomes a pure map lookup.

  Same idea applies to layout_of: consumer registers type defs (with field-types expressed in terms of type params), facade substitutes concrete args and computes layouts itself at query time — no
  callback. mir_shims same story (consumer registers destructor symbol, facade handles the glue). upstream_monomorphizations_for is already near-pure.

  The real constraint: tcx only exists inside rustc_driver::run_compiler's lifetime, and after_analysis is the first lifecycle hook where type resolution works. So "up front" means "inside after_analysis,
   before mono." That's fine but worth naming — "pre-computed" doesn't mean before run_compiler, it means before the mono phase within the run.

  There's precedent in the codebase: B6's populate_toylang_instances_from_cgus is exactly this pattern applied to internal-callee discovery. Generalizing it is natural.

  Non-obvious gotcha: layout_of fires extremely high volume, including derived forms (Option<YourType>, &YourType, etc.). The "pre-compute a map" version has to answer arbitrary post-mono type queries,
  not just pre-enumerated ones. You'd need the consumer's type-reconstruction logic to run inside the facade's query provider, using registered data — which IS possible but it's registration-driven logic,
   not a static lookup.

  Q2: symbol-name generation without calling consumer

  Yes, cleanly. notify_concrete_entry_point_inner post-B6 is already just format!("__toylang_impl_{}{}", name, mangled_args) plus a log push. It's algorithmic mangling, not semantic business logic.
  Consumer declares a naming scheme once:

  pub struct SymbolScheme {
      pub prefix: String,
      pub type_arg_separator: String,
      pub mangle_type: fn(&ResolvedType) -> String,  // pure fn, not a callback with state
  }

  Facade owns the mangling after registration. No per-Instance callback. The consumer's symbol logic becomes configuration instead of control flow.

  Small caveat: mangle_type still has to be some kind of function the consumer provides, because type mangling depends on the consumer's type system. But it's a pure fn, not a Callbacks method with &mut
  state — same distinction as LangPredicates vs LangCallbacks in our trait split.

  The broader framing

  Your instinct is right: most of what currently fires during mono is "facade asking consumer for information that could have been registered up front." The actual per-Instance work rustc does (walking
  the synthetic MIR body, substituting Params, codegen-ing Rust deps) doesn't require consumer callbacks — it requires consumer data. Separating "data declared up front" from "control flow during mono" is
   the refactor.

  What would remain consumer-callback-driven after this shift:
  - generate_and_compile — the consumer's own LLVM backend runs here, for consumer-owned code. Not a rustc→consumercallback; it's orchestration the facade hands to the consumer. Keeps.
  - after_analysis itself — consumer populates the maps here. This is the one and only mono-prep hook. Arguably the only callback the facade really needs.

  End state: erw is a library that exposes "register these things, give me an after_analysis hook to prep my data, call generate_and_compile for my own codegen." The mono phase becomes pure facade
  internals. That matches your "library-ish" goal.

  Scope feel: meaningful refactor, not a rewrite. Maybe 1–2 weeks. Doable because the pieces are already well-separated — you'd be moving logic between trait methods, not redesigning the architecture.
  Biggest risk is the layout_of high-volume path (Q1 gotcha) — wants careful design before coding.

  Want to turn any of this into a design doc / investigation plan, or just sitting with the sketch for now?

✻ Cooked for 1m 25s

❯ after_analysis sg. tell me more about layout_of

⏺ Here's the layout_of story in detail.

  Why it seems hard

  High volume is a red herring — rustc's query system memoizes, so the number of distinct consumer-type instantiations is the same whether we answer via callback or map lookup. Performance isn't the
  constraint.

  The real constraint: we don't know which concrete instantiations rustc will demand until mono runs. Point<i32>, Point<String>, Point<Vec<Foo>> might all be needed; or only the first; or some subset we
  can't predict. So "pre-compute a map keyed by concrete type" is infeasible — we'd have to enumerate every possible instantiation, which is exactly the work rustc's mono collector does.

  Why it's actually easy once you see the trick

  The same trick that makes optimized_mir work applies here verbatim: return data with Params intact, let rustc's substitution engine do the per-instantiation work.

  Concretely: in after_analysis, consumer walks its registry. For each registered struct Point<T, U>, it resolves the fields to Ty<'tcx> values with TyKind::Param(0) / Param(1) in place of T, U. Registers
   with the facade:

  Point → { type_params: 2, field_templates: [Param(0), Vec<Param(1)>] }

  That's one entry per consumer type, populated once, never touched again.

  When facade's layout_of override fires for Point<i32, String>:

  1. Look up Point in the registered table.
  2. Call tcx.mk_args(&[i32, String]) to build the arg set.
  3. Instantiate each field template: GenericArgs::instantiate(tcx, field_template, args) → concrete Vec<String>.
  4. Call tcx.layout_of(Vec<String>) on each field (rustc handles the recursion; not our problem).
  5. Compute the C-style struct layout from the field layouts.

  No consumer callback fires. The substitution uses rustc's own machinery, same as optimized_mir's Param substitution, same as every generic Rust function's codegen.

  What stays consumer-specific

  The field-template resolution itself. Converting the consumer's ResolvedType::RustType("Vec") with type_args: [TypeParam("T")] into a rustc Ty<'tcx> of Vec<Param(0)> requires the consumer's oracle —
  specifically the find_rust_type_def_id walk over __lang_stubs re-exports. But that walk happens once per consumer type in after_analysis, not per query. So the consumer provides one "resolve my types"
  pass; the facade owns every query afterward.

  The consumer's surface narrows to something like:

  fn resolve_types<'tcx>(&self, tcx: TyCtxt<'tcx>) -> RegisteredTypes<'tcx>;

  Called once, in after_analysis. Returns a bundle the facade owns for the rest of the compile.

  Real gotchas

  Three I'd flag:

  1. Early-bound lifetimes (per @ELASZ). If a consumer type has lifetime-parameterized fields (Ref<'a, T>), the registered template needs re_erased synthesized in the lifetime slots. Doable — the existing
   build_generic_args_for_item helper already does this for methods; same pattern applies at the type-registration step.
  2. Dependent field types. A field of Point<T> could be HashMap<T, Vec<T>>. The template must be a single Ty<'tcx> with Params deep inside. Rustc's ty::TyKind already supports this — you build it
  recursively during resolution. Mechanical, not deep.
  3. Tech debt #30 interaction. The eprintln! the layout-probe tests assert on currently fires inside the query provider (cache-skippable). In the new design, that log emission moves to the
  registration-time walk, making it cache-deterministic. This refactor actually fixes tech debt #30 as a side effect — the query provider becomes pure, and the logging happens at a deterministic up-front
  moment. Bonus.

  Non-gotcha (worth naming)

  You might worry about "what if rustc asks for layout_of(Point<UnknownType>) where UnknownType is something the consumer hasn't registered?" — that never happens. Consumer types are only instantiable
  with types the consumer's source code mentions, which means those types are already in its registry (per @RTMEIZ). The substitution path only ever hands back types rustc can compute layout_of for.

  Scope feel

  Narrower than I first suggested. The trick is exactly parallel to optimized_mir's, which is shipped and stable. Biggest work is the one-time type-resolution pass that converts the consumer's
  ResolvedType templates to rustc Ty templates with Params. Maybe 3–4 days including the tech-debt-#30 side fix andupdating the layout-probe tests to assert on after_analysis-time emission.

  Want me to sketch what the consumer-facing API would actually look like (the RegisteredTypes struct, the resolve_types trait method, what gets retired from LangCallbacks)?

✻ Sautéed for 1m 22s

❯ qq: we dont have to tell rustc about our internal toylang types, right? those are opaque to rustc? so often we really just have to tell rustc about byte arrays instead or something with the right size?

⏺ Your intuition is exactly right, and actually goes further than you stated. You don't need byte arrays — for most consumer types, you can just put the real field types in the stub struct and delete the
  layout_of override entirely.

  The current setup is weirder than it needs to be

  Today, the stub looks like:

  pub struct Pair<A, B>(std::marker::PhantomData<(A, B)>);  // opaque, 0 real fields

  Rustc thinks Pair<A, B> is a ZST. We override layout_of to say "actually it's 8 bytes." This creates a mismatch that rustc's debuginfo walker and other introspection paths have tripped over (the
  stub_gen comments document the pain: "source field count must match the layout-reported field count; mismatch ICEs").

  If the stub were instead:

  #[repr(C)]
  pub struct Pair<A, B>(A, B);  // real fields

  ...rustc computes the layout itself. For Pair<i32, i64>, #[repr(C)] gives exactly the same layout our monomorphize_type + build_layout code computes today. No override needed.

  What becomes deletable

  - layout_of override — gone. Rustc computes layout from source.
  - monomorphize_type callback — gone. Its only job was feeding the override.
  - MonomorphizeTypeResult struct — gone.
  - B6's re-entrancy dance around stateless monomorphize_type — gone (no callback to worry about re-entry on).
  - Tech debt #30 (cache-fragile eprintln!) — gone (logging moves to the after_analysis pre-pass).
  - mir_shims override for drop glue — partially gone. If the stub struct has real fields, rustc generates real drop glue automatically. Custom destructors (__toylang_drop_Foo) would still want an
  override, but the default path disappears for types that don't have Drop impls.

  That's ~250 LoC of facade code plus ~100 LoC of consumer code, potentially.

  What stays consumer-specific

  stub_gen gets a bit more work: it has to emit real field types, which means recursively resolving consumer-type references (if Point has field Pair<i32, T>, the stub needs Pair declared and re-exports
  in order). Topological sort over the type registry — mechanical, bounded.

  Also: generic type parameters with no field usage (struct Foo<T> { x: i32 } — T unused in fields) still need PhantomData<T> to satisfy rustc's "all generics must be used" rule. Minor wart, not a
  blocker.

  Real constraints

  1. #[repr(C)] pins layout. Toylang's internal codegen already assumes C-style layout (per llvm_gen.rs), so this matches. A consumer whose type system disagrees with C layout would need #[repr(Rust)] +
  accept rustc's field reordering, or keep the override path.
  2. Consumer types need to be expressible in Rust's type system. For toylang: fine — toylang's types are a subset. For a consumer with exotic type-system features rustc can't name (linear types? effect
  polymorphism? regions that aren't lifetimes?), you'd need to keep the opaque-struct + layout_of override path. The override isn't useless; it's a fallback.
  3. The fields become visible in debuginfo. Currently they're opaque blobs; if you surface them, gdb/lldb can see Pair<i32, i64> as two real fields. Probably a feature, not a bug.
  4. Drop behavior. If you use real fields, rustc runs real Drop on them when the outer type is dropped. Toylang's values are mostly POD so this is fine — but if a consumer-owned type has a field that's a
   Vec<T> and consumer semantics want to skip Rust's Drop, you'd need ManuallyDrop wrappers or keep the override path for that type.

  Scope feel

  This is actually bigger than the "pre-computed after_analysis" refactor because it touches the stub generator, not just the facade. But the architectural simplification is larger — you're deleting whole
   extension points, not just moving logic between them.

  If we did this, the facade's mono-phase surface would collapse from "six query overrides + consumer callbacks during mono" to "three query overrides (optimized_mir, symbol_name, collect_and_partition),
  two of which will become map-lookups after the after_analysis refactor." The fourth (mir_shims) survives only forcustom destructors.

  That's a strikingly small footprint for "embed a custom language in rustc."

  Worth sketching as a separate investigation? Or would you rather bundle it with the after_analysis refactor into one bigger design doc?

✻ Sautéed for 1m 47s

❯ but i dont want to tell rust what's inside

⏺ Then stay with opacity — my previous answer overstepped. The real-fields approach does break opacity (rustc can see the field layout via debuginfo, introspection, etc.). Throw that suggestion out.

  What opacity requires

  You need to tell rustc size and alignment — that's non-negotiable, rustc uses it for every ABI decision, every Vec<YourType> allocation, every memcpy. You do NOT need to tell rustc what's inside. The
  current opaque-stub + layout_of override preserves exactly that:

  - Stub: pub struct Pair<A, B>(PhantomData<(A, B)>); — looks like a ZST in source
  - Override: "actually 24 bytes, 8-byte align, FieldsShape with zero visible fields"

  Rustc treats it as an opaque blob. Can't index fields, can't do reordering, can't introspect. That's the property you want. Keep it.

  Then what changes?

  The layout_of override stays — the callback to consumer code at query time is what gets cut. Today's flow is:

  1. Rustc fires layout_of(Pair<i32, String>)
  2. Override calls call_monomorphize_type("Pair", tcx, concrete_ty) — consumer callback
  3. Consumer returns vec![i32, String]
  4. Facade computes size=24 from those field types

  New flow:

  1. In after_analysis (one-time), consumer walks its registry. For each type, builds a Vec<Ty<'tcx>> of field templates with TyKind::Param(i) in the slots where consumer type-params go. Hands the facade
  HashMap<String, FieldTemplates<'tcx>>.
  2. Rustc fires layout_of(Pair<i32, String>)
  3. Override looks up "Pair" in the map, gets [Param(0), Param(1)]
  4. Facade calls GenericArgs::instantiate(tcx, template, concrete_args) → [i32, String]
  5. Computes size=24

  Step 2–5 is pure facade code, no consumer callback. Rustc sees the same answer either way. The consumer-to-facade contract shifts from "answer per query" to "register data once, facade answers from the
  data."

  Opacity to rustc: identical to today. The change is entirely internal to the facade/consumer boundary.

  What actually gets deleted

  - call_monomorphize_type dispatcher + its stateless contract
  - MonomorphizeTypeResult struct
  - The B6 re-entrancy workaround (no callback = no re-entry risk)
  - Roughly half of monomorphize_type's consumer implementation

  What stays:
  - layout_of override in the facade — still intercepts, still reports opaque layout
  - Stub generation — still emits PhantomData<(A, B)> for generics, unit structs for non-generics
  - Everything rustc sees

  Scope feel

  Smaller than the real-fields refactor, genuinely bounded. The consumer adds one pre-pass (walk the registry, build field-template map in after_analysis) and loses one callback method. Facade's layout_of
   gets a few lines simpler (map lookup + substitute instead of callback). The architecture shifts from "control flow into consumer during mono" toward "data handed to facade up front, mono reads data
  only" — which is your library-ness goal, applied surgically to this one surface.

  Opacity kept. Callback eliminated. Two wins, no cost visible to rustc.

✻ Brewed for 1m 2s

❯ whats the Vec<Ty<'tcx>> inside the struct?

⏺ Rustc Ty<'tcx> values — fully-formed types in rustc's type system, but with TyKind::Param(idx) in the slots where consumer type parameters go. idx is the position of that parameter in the consumer
  type's declaration (0 for the first type-param, 1 for the second, etc.).

  Worked examples

  Consumer declaration — rustc Ty representation (what goes in the Vec):

  struct Point { x: i32, y: i32 } (non-generic)
  [ tcx.types.i32,
    tcx.types.i32 ]

  struct Pair<A, B> { first: A, second: B }
  [ Ty::new_param(tcx, 0, "A"),
    Ty::new_param(tcx, 1, "B") ]

  struct Wrapper<T> { items: Vec<T>, count: i32 }
  [ Ty::new_adt(tcx, Vec_adt_def, &[Param(0), Global]),
    tcx.types.i32 ]

  struct Nested<T, U> { pair: Pair<T, U>, tag: i32 } (consumer type nested in consumer type)
  [ Ty::new_adt(tcx, Pair_adt_def, &[Param(0), Param(1)]),
    tcx.types.i32 ]

  Each element is a real Ty<'tcx> rustc knows how to reason about. The Param holes are the only thing that makes them "generic" — otherwise they're identical to the types rustc would construct if it were
  doing layout itself.

  How the substitution step works

  When rustc fires layout_of(Wrapper<i64>):

  1. Extract the concrete args from the input type: [i64]
  2. Wrap as GenericArgs: tcx.mk_args(&[i64])
  3. Instantiate each template:
    - tcx.instantiate(Vec<Param(0), Global>, args) → Vec<i64, Global>
    - tcx.instantiate(i32, args) → i32 (no Params, unchanged)
  4. Feed concrete field types to build_layout (existing code, unchanged)

  The substitution is GenericArgs::instantiate, rustc's own substitution machinery. It's the same function rustc's mono collector calls when walking a generic Rust function's MIR.

  The symmetry with optimized_mir

  This is the same pattern optimized_mir already uses, one level over. Today:

  - optimized_mir returns a synthetic MIR body with Param placeholders in the dep-reference args; rustc's mono collector substitutes Params per caller when walking the body.
  - Proposed: layout_of reads a pre-registered vec of field types with Param placeholders; facade substitutes Params per concrete instantiation before computing layout.

  Same Params, same substitution engine, same "don't pre-enumerate, let the substitution machinery do per-instantiation work" design. The only difference is one is per-caller (collector's walk), one is
  per-concrete-type (layout query).

  That symmetry is actually a good sign — reusing an established pattern in the codebase rather than inventing a new one.

✻ Sautéed for 35s

❯ but does that mean we're telling rust the number of types inside? i really only ever want to tell it the size and alignment

⏺ No — rustc never sees the field templates. They live in facade-internal state.

  What actually crosses the rustc boundary

  Consumer registry (after_analysis):
    Pair<A,B> → [Param(0), Param(1)]      ← lives in facade HashMap
                                               (facade-internal)
         ↓
    layout_of(Pair<i32, String>) fires
         ↓
    Facade substitutes: [i32, String]     ← ephemeral, never persisted
    Facade computes:    offset 0 + offset 8 → size=32, align=8
         ↓
    Return LayoutData {
        size:  32,
        align: 8,
        fields: FieldsShape::Arbitrary {
            offsets: [],            // ← ZERO entries
            memory_index: [],       // ← ZERO entries
        },
        backend_repr: BackendRepr::Memory { sized: true },
        ...
    }
         ↓
    Rustc sees: opaque blob, 32 bytes, 8-aligned, 0 visible fields

  The templates are intermediate data we use to derive size/align. We hand rustc the same LayoutData shape we hand it today — FieldsShape with an empty offsets vec, BackendRepr::Memory { sized: true }.
  Field count reported: zero. Field types reported: none. Offsets reported: none.

  The only thing rustc ever learns

  - Size (yes — it has to, for ABI)
  - Alignment (yes — same reason)
  - "Opaque memory blob" (yes — via BackendRepr::Memory)
  - "Zero fields to introspect" (yes — via empty FieldsShape::Arbitrary)

  That's it. The templates existing in our HashMap doesn't change any of this; they're just our computational substrate for arriving at the size/align numbers.

  If anything, this is stricter opacity than the current callback-based design, because the templates are static data instead of something computed by consumer code that could theoretically leak info
  through side effects. Same rustc-visible surface; fewer moving parts on our side.

✻ Baked for 34s

※ recap: You're exploring how to make erw more library-ish by cutting rustc→consumer callbacks during mono; the current sketch moves layout_of to pre-computed field templates registered in after_analysis,
   preserving opacity. Next: decide whether to turn this into a design doc or keep sketching.

     docs/historical/handoff-codegen-backend-plugin.md:438:After sub-stage 4d: same suite passes using vanilla nightly-2025-01-15 (no fork).
     docs/historical/handoff-codegen-backend-plugin.md:473:After 4d, `git log` on your main branch should have fourstage-4 sub-commits. The rustc-fork directory at `~/rust` should be at vanilla
     nightly-2025-01-15 with no diffs. The toolchain `rustc-fork` can be uninstalled:
     docs/historical/handoff-codegen-backend-plugin.md:477:rustup toolchain install nightly-2025-01-15
     docs/historical/handoff-codegen-backend-plugin.md:480:The full test suite should pass with `cargo +nightly-2025-01-15 test -p toylangc` (modulo `rust-toolchain.toml` pinning).

      with codegen-skip + visibility-override hooks."
     docs/historical/phase-history.md:197:- **4d** (`c25aa4b`): toolchain switch (vanilla `nightly-2025-01-15`) + full doc pass + handoff move to historical.
     docs/historical/handoff-two-crate-migration.md:61:3. **Zero-fork architecture.** As of stage 4 (commit `c25aa4b`), erw builds against vanilla `nightly-2025-01-15` with no rustc fork patches. All
     rustc integration flows through `Config::override_queries` + `FileLoader` + a `CodegenBackend` wrapper. Stage 5 preserves zero-fork; we're not touching rustc internals.

  ---
  Section 2: Known API drift (confirmed by PR dates)

  2a. PR #135880 — RunCompiler removed (merged 2025-01-24)

  Two call sites will fail to compile:

  ┌────────────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │             File:Line              │                            Current                             │            Becomes                        │
  ├────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ rustc-lang-facade/src/driver.rs:52 │ rustc_driver::RunCompiler::new(rustc_args, &mut driver).run(); │ rustc_driver::run_compiler(rustc_args, &mut driver); │
  ├────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ toylangc/src/main.rs:194           │ rustc_driver::RunCompiler::new(args, &mut cb).run();           │ rustc_driver::run_compiler(args, &mut cb);           │
  └────────────────────────────────────┴────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Cost: ~15 min. Trivial signature change, no logic rework.

  2b. PR #141769 — codegen_crate signature narrowed (merged 2025-06-16)

  One trait impl must change shape:

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┬────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                       File                       │       Method       │           Change                                                     │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ rustc-lang-facade/src/codegen_wrapper.rs:72-77   │ codegen_crate      │ Drop metadata: EncodedMetadata and need_metadata_module: bool params                                          │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ rustc-lang-facade/src/codegen_wrapper.rs:99      │ codegen_crate body │ self.inner.codegen_crate(tcx, metadata,need_metadata_module) → self.inner.codegen_crate(tcx)                 │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ rustc-lang-facade/src/codegen_wrapper.rs:141-148 │ link               │ Add metadata: EncodedMetadata parameter, forward to self.inner.link(sess, codegen_results, metadata, outputs) │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────┴────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Cost: ~30 min. Trivial once signatures are known; the forwarding pattern preserves all existing logic.

  2c. Callbacks trait additions (non-breaking on current nightly)

  after_crate_root_parsing and after_expansion were added to rustc_driver::Callbacks with default impls. Our impls at driver.rs:57 and main.rs:188 only override config / after_analysis — no code change
  required.

  Cost: 0 min. Mentioned for completeness.

  2d. ParamEnv → TypingEnv (merged 2024-11-19, predates our pin)

  Our codebase already uses TypingEnv::fully_monomorphized(). No change.

  2e. Abi → BackendRepr (merged 2024-11-01, predates our pin)

  Our codebase already uses BackendRepr. No change.

  ---
  Section 3: Unknown-drift surfaces (expect 1–3 weeks of repair)

  Per risks.md, these are the dominant cost centers. Exact drift unknown until the new nightly is checked; order ofprobability:

  3a. MIR construction (risks.md B3 — "~100% per bump")

  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                      File                      │          Function          │                      Risk                                                          │
  ├────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ rustc-lang-facade/src/queries/optimized_mir.rs │ build_dependency_body      │ High. Constructs Statement, Terminator, Rvalue::Cast, CastKind::PointerCoercion(ReifyFnPointer, CoercionSource),      │
  │                                                │ (~110 LoC)                 │ NullOp::SizeOf, SourceInfo, ClearCrossCrate. Every one of these has drifted in prior bumps.                           │
  ├────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ rustc-lang-facade/src/mir_helpers.rs           │ build_drop_call_body (~100 │ High. TerminatorKind::Call, Spanned, UnwindAction, CallSource, Body::new. Same drift surface.                         │
  │                                                │  LoC)                      │                                                                                    │
  ├────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ rustc-lang-facade/src/queries/drop_glue.rs     │ dispatch logic             │ Low. Pattern-matches InstanceKind::DropGlue — enum variant name has been stable.                                      │
  └────────────────────────────────────────────────┴────────────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Cost: 3–7 days realistically. Repair loop: cargo check → adapt a signature → repeat. -Zvalidate-mir is your friend here; budget time for it catching things the compile step misses.

  3b. ABI helpers (risks.md B4 — "15–25% per 5yr, but accumulates")

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                 File                 │                              Surface                              │                                     Risk                                           │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ rustc-lang-facade/src/abi_helpers.rs │ CoercedReturn, CoercedParam, cast_target_to_llvm_str,             │ Medium. Pattern-matches PassMode, BackendRepr, rustc_abi::Primitive, Reg, RegKind,       │
  │                                      │ primitive_to_llvm_str, reg_to_llvm_str                            │ CastTarget. PassMode variants have split before.                                         │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ toylangc/src/llvm_gen.rs             │ Consumers of the above at every Rust-method call site             │ Low-medium. If abi_helpers.rs repairs cleanly, llvm_gen follows. If they introduce new   │
  │                                      │                                                                   │ variants we don't handle, more work.                                                     │
  └──────────────────────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Cost: 2–5 days. Usually localized — a single PassMode variant change is a handful of match arms.

  3c. Partitioner / CGU (risks.md B2, B5)

  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                    File                    │                Surface                │                          Risk                                                       │
  ├────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │                                            │                                       │ Medium. Relies on MonoItemData's public shape + the "plugin's post-partition mutation survives to LLVM          │
  │ rustc-lang-facade/src/queries/partition.rs │ lang_collect_and_partition_mono_items │ emission" timing assumption. If internalization phase timing shifts, Phase-6 unwrap tests fail with link        │
  │                                            │                                       │ errors.                                                                                     │
  ├────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ rustc-lang-facade/src/cgu_stash.rs         │ CodegenUnit<'tcx> pointer stashing    │ Medium. Raw-pointer + lifetime-erasure trick. If rustc changes CodegenUnit's arena semantics, the stash needs   │
  │                                            │                                       │ redesign.                                                                                     │
  └────────────────────────────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Cost: Best case 0 days (stays working); worst case 1–2 weeks (retreat to fork hook per B2 §Reaction). First teststo fail: Phase-6 test_option_unwrap_basic, test_result_unwrap_basic,
  test_unwrap_arithmetic_chain.

  3d. Query provider signatures (risks.md C3)

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                                   Surface                                                   │                                      Risk                                          │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ layout_of, symbol_name, optimized_mir, mir_shims, collect_and_partition_mono_items,                         │ Low. These are the most stable part of the extension surface. Key/return shape has    │
  │ upstream_monomorphizations_for typedefs in queries/*.rs                                                     │ been stable for multiple nightlies.                                                   │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  LayoutOfFn in queries/layout.rs:34-37 is the most likely to drift — its comment explicitly warns that the input type has shifted before.

  ---
  Section 4: Proposed execution order

  Once you commit to bumping, execute in this order (don't interleave):

  1. Snapshot: note current test count (210), run cargo check → clean baseline.
  2. Update canonical pin: edit rust-toolchain.toml:2. Install the new toolchain. Don't update code-level strings yet.
  3. Install rust-src and rustc-dev: rustup component add --toolchain nightly-2026-01-15 rustc-dev rust-src llvm-tools-preview.
  4. Compile loop — facade first: cargo check -p rustc-lang-facade. Fix everything under rustc-lang-facade/src/ in order: §2a, §2b, §3a, §3b, §3c. Run until clean.
  5. Compile loop — consumer: cargo check -p toylangc. Fix llvm_gen.rs, callbacks_impl.rs, oracle.rs — whatever broke from facade changes cascading in.
  6. Update hardcoded strings: §1b (7 code sites) — recommend introducing const TOYLANG_NIGHTLY during this bump tomake future bumps trivial.
  7. Unit tests: cargo test -p toylangc --bins. Target: 67/67.
  8. Integration tests: cargo test -p toylangc --test integration_projects. Target: 128/128. If Phase-6 unwrap tests fail, you hit §3c — escalate per risks.md B2.
  9. Standalone tests: cargo test -p toylangc --test standalone_tests. Target: 15/15.
  10. Doc sweep: §1c (21 sites). Update risks.md §280 with the empirical cost of this bump so the next maintainer has real data.
  11. HANDOFF-TL.md: bump the pin reference in §1, §4, §8, §9 sanity check.
  12. Commit as single PR — not spread across work.

  ---
  Section 5: Cost estimate

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬──────────────┐
  │                      Component                      │    Budget    │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────┤
  │ §2a + §2b + §2c + §1a-b (known drift + pin updates) │ ~1 day       │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────┤
  │ §3a MIR construction                                │ 3–7 days     │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────┤
  │ §3b ABI helpers                                     │ 2–5 days     │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────┤
  │ §3c partitioner (best case)                         │ 0–1 day      │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────┤
  │ §3c partitioner (worst case — B2 fires)             │ +1–2 weeks   │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────┤
  │ Tests + doc sweep                                   │ 1–2 days     │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────┤
  │ Total — expected                                    │ ~1.5–2 weeks │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────┤
  │ Total — if B2 fires                                 │ ~3–4 weeks   │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴──────────────┘

  Aligns with risks.md B3 budget ("~1 week per 6-month bump" × 2.5 bumps = 2.5 weeks).

  ---
  Section 6: Pre-commit go/no-go

  Go if:
  - You have 2 dedicated weeks without feature work
  - You're prepared to escalate to a small fork if B2 fires (risks.md §6)
  - The architectural reward (clean slate, no future compound drift) matches your hygiene priority

  Defer if:
  - Vale replies and needs engagement first
  - You'd rather start the upstream LlvmCodegenBackend PR (§3b of HANDOFF)
  - You don't have 2 consecutive weeks of focus

  Your call. Ready to execute if you want, or happy to leave the punch list as a pre-flight artifact for whenever.

  Structure

  12 sections, matching the project's junior-handoff house style:

  1. Metadata header — risk level (medium), budget (1.5–2 wk expected, 3–4 wk worst), final state
  2. Context — why, what prompted it, intended outcome
  3. Required reading — 3–4 hours, ordered by priority, risks.md §B2/B3/B4/B5 flagged as most important pre-bump doc
  4. Pin occurrences — exact file:line for all 33 sites, classified into 4 tiers (canonical/code/docs/historical-don't-touch)
  5. Known API drift — the 3 confirmed PR changes with copy-paste-ready before/after diffs at exact file:line
  6. Unknown drift surfaces — the 4 risks.md cost-centers with canary tests and repair strategies
  7. Implementation steps — 16 staged sub-phases, each a commit point with test expectations
  8. Critical subtleties — 10 items covering things that will bite (cache contamination, +pin habits, B6 walk, the DPSFDOZ and GCMLZ arcana)
  9. Verification — exact test-count expectations, the grep that confirms no pin strings remain
  10. Out of scope — 9 things explicitly forbidden (features, refactoring, Vale, fork introduction)
  11. If stuck — escalation gates explicitly list §5.3 (partitioner/B2) as "contact TL immediately, do NOT fork unilaterally"; self-recovery tactics for the non-escalation cases
  12. Rollback + one-paragraph recap

  Escalation gate

  Per your instruction, §5.3 and §10.1 both state the B2 escalation rule explicitly:

  ▎ If §5.3 fires (Phase-6 link errors after the bump): do NOT retreat to a fork on your own initiative. Send the diagnostic packet described in §5.3. This is a project-shape decision, not a junior
  ▎ decision.

  The diagnostic packet is pre-specified (test names, linker output, git log, relevant PR search) so the escalationis actionable the moment it fires.

  Ready to hand to a junior whenever you want.

  doc-tracking of that send is its own change. My recommendation: commit them as one focused commit before pushing — leaving them uncommitted indefinitely is awkward and the next doc sweep will compound.

  Suggested message: Record Vale response sent 2026-04-20.

  2. Cache-fragility caveat

  The junior flagged this as pre-existing, not a bump regression. Worth filing as a tech-debt entry (#30?) so it doesn't get lost. Symptom is specific: t_of_r_layout / t_of_t_layout after running --test X
   followed by the full suite on a shared target dir. Fix is wiping caches. The root cause is probably in the eprintln! emission path in layout.rs being incremental-skippable (same class of issue as B6
  but different symptom, since the populate walk in generate_and_compile doesn't cover layout-log emission).

  Want me to draft the tech-debt entry?

  3. Final PR

  Handoff §6.16 said "one PR, descriptive title." 10 commits are staged but not pushed. Before pushing I'd:
  - Commit the Vale edits (above)
  - Re-verify full 210/210 on a clean rebuild to rule out cache-fragility false-greens (the junior did this, but the caveat flags that it can flake — worth a fresh run)
  - Confirm the target branch (main vs a feature branch)

  Want me to proceed?

⏺ Step 2: Draft tech debt #30

⏺ Read(/Users/verdagon/erw/docs/architecture/known-tech-debt.md · lines 78-117)
  ⎿  Read 40 lines

⏺ Update(/Users/verdagon/erw/docs/architecture/known-tech-debt.md)
  ⎿  Added 62 lines
      112
      113  ---
      114
      115 +## 30. `layout_of` log-emission cache-skip under shared target dir
      116 +
      117 +### Problem
      118 +
      119 +Layout-probe integration tests (`t_of_r_layout`, `t_of_t_layout`, and
      120 +the siblings that assert on the `[toylang] layout_of intercepted for:
      121 +… size=N align=M` stderr line from `rustc-lang-facade/src/queries/layout.rs`)
      122 +can spuriously fail when:
      123 +
      124 +1. The suite is run piecemeal (e.g. `cargo test --test integration_projects`
      125 +   followed by `cargo test -p toylangc`) against the shared
      126 +   `CARGO_TARGET_DIR` at `toylangc/target/integration-projects-cache/`, and
      127 +2. The earlier run warmed rustc's per-item incremental cache for the
      128 +   same project's layout queries.
      129 +
      130 +On the second run the `lang_layout_of` override never fires (cache hit
      131 +at the query-provider layer), so the `eprintln!` that the layout-probe
      132 +tests assert on is never emitted, and the test fails with "expected
      133 +`…size=8 align=4` in build stderr."
      134 +
      135 +Wiping `toylangc/target/integration-projects-cache/` and each per-project
      136 +`.toylang-build/` dir restores 210/210. The failure is deterministic given
      137 +the warm-cache precondition, not flaky.
      138 +
      139 +### Why it's distinct from B6
      140 +
      141 +Same *class* of problem as risks.md §B6 (query-provider side-effect
      142 +fragility under rustc's incremental cache), different *site*. B6 was
      143 +about `state.toylang_instances` population — the fix moved that to an
      144 +up-front deterministic walk in `generate_and_compile` (`populate_toylang_instances_from_cgus`).
      145 +The `layout_of` log emission is still a side-effect of the query
      146 +provider firing; it wasn't moved because no test depended on its
      147 +cache-deterministic emission until layout probes were added.
      148 +
      149 +Diagnosed 2026-04-22 during the nightly-2025-01-15 → nightly-2026-01-20
      150 +bump's verification pass. Pre-existing; not a bump regression (reproduces
      151 +on the old pin too).
      152 +
      153 +### Fix
      154 +
      155 +Two viable approaches, both bounded:
      156 +
      157 +1. **Move layout logging to the `generate_and_compile` walk.** Iterate
      158 +   `state.toylang_instances` + the registry's consumer types once up
      159 +   front, call `call_monomorphize_type` for each concrete consumer type
      160 +   (it's stateless + cheap), and emit the log line from there. Parallel
      161 +   to B6's architectural fix. The layout-probe tests would assert on
      162 +   the deterministic emission instead of the query-provider one.
      163 +2. **Have the test harness wipe the shared cache between test binaries.**
      164 +   Cheaper but only shifts the fragility — other future tests that depend
      165 +   on query-side-effect stderr emission would hit the same wall.
      166 +
      167 +Option 1 is the real fix. Option 2 is a workaround if layout-probe
      168 +tests need to stay green in the interim.
      169 +
      170 +Non-blocking; the user-facing `toylangc build` flow doesn't hit this
      171 +(users don't share `CARGO_TARGET_DIR` across unrelated compilations the
      172 +way the test harness does). Flagged for whoever next touches the
      173 +layout-probe or integration-test harness.
      174 +
      175 +---
      176 +
      177  ## Resolved Items (sessions 1–8)
      178
      179  | # | Item | Session | Resolution |

⏺ Bash(git add docs/architecture/known-tech-debt.md && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
      Document tech debt #30: `layout_of` log-emission cache-skip under shared target dir.

      Flagged by the nightly-2025-01-15 → nightly-2026-01-20 bump's verification
      pass as a pre-existing symptom that reproduces on the old pin too.
      Same class of problem as risks.md §B6 (query-provider side-effect
      fragility under rustc's incremental cache) but a distinct site — the
      `[toylang] layout_of intercepted for: ... size=N align=M` stderr
      emission that layout-probe tests (`t_of_r_layout`, `t_of_t_layout`,
      and siblings) assert on.

      Documents the two viable fixes: (1) architectural — move logging into
      `generate_and_compile`'s walk, parallel to B6's fix; (2) workaround —
      have the test harness wipe the shared cache between binaries.

      Non-blocking; user-facing `toylangc build` doesn't hit this. Only the
      test harness's shared `CARGO_TARGET_DIR` setup triggers it.

      Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
      EOF
      )")
  ⎿  [main 8b4c21e] Document tech debt #30: `layout_of` log-emission cache-skip under shared target dir.

⏺ rustc Extension Points — A Step-by-Step Guide

  Scoped to the rustc hooks this project actually touches, in roughly the order they fire. Accurate as of our current pin (nightly-2026-01-20).

  Step 0: The gate — #![feature(rustc_private)]

  All of rustc's internal crates (rustc_driver, rustc_interface, rustc_middle, rustc_codegen_ssa, rustc_codegen_llvm, rustc_monomorphize, rustc_hashes, ...) are nightly-only and gated by this feature. A
  crate that depends on them declares it at the crate root. There's no SemVer; APIs drift across nightlies. It's the "all bets off" escape hatch.

  You also need to link against the compiler's shared libraries at runtime. The idiomatic path is $(rustc --print sysroot)/lib — that's where librustc_driver-*.dylib/.so lives. Two ways to satisfy it:

  - Env var — set DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH (macOS) or LD_LIBRARY_PATH (Linux). Fine for shells and harnesses.
  - rpath at link time — cargo:rustc-link-arg=-Wl,-rpath,$(rustc --print sysroot)/lib in build.rs. More robust for distributed binaries.

  Step 1: The driver entry point — rustc_driver::run_compiler

  rustc_driver::run_compiler(rustc_args, &mut my_callbacks);

  rustc_args is a &[String] in rustc argv shape (skip argv[0]; the rest is whatever rustc CLI would accept). my_callbacks is any type implementing rustc_driver::Callbacks. This was a builder called
  RunCompiler::new(...).run() in earlier nightlies; PR #135880 replaced it with the free function we use now.

  Small siblings:
  - rustc_driver::install_ice_hook(report_url, |_| {}) — panic hook that prints a "please report at " on ICE.
  - rustc_driver::catch_with_exit_code(|| { ... }) — maps rustc's panic/early-exit patterns into a clean process exit code.

  rustc_driver itself is an almost-empty re-export shim; all the code lives in rustc_driver_impl (that's what you see in stack traces). The split exists so rustc_driver_impl can compile in parallel with
  the rest of the compiler. As a consumer you depend on rustc_driver.

  The canonical reference drivers, if you need to see how people actually use this trait in anger: clippy_driver (in src/tools/clippy/ of the rust-lang/rust tree) overrides lints via Callbacks::config;
  miri (in src/tools/miri/) overrides MIR and the codegen step; the dev-guide maintains a minimal example at rustc-dev-guide/examples/rustc-driver-example.rs.

  Step 2: The lifecycle hooks — rustc_driver::Callbacks

  trait Callbacks {
      fn config(&mut self, config: &mut Config) {}
      fn after_crate_root_parsing(&mut self, compiler: &Compiler, krate: &mut Crate) -> Compilation { ... }
      fn after_expansion<'tcx>(&mut self, compiler: &Compiler, tcx: TyCtxt<'tcx>) -> Compilation { ... }
      fn after_analysis<'tcx>(&mut self, compiler: &Compiler, tcx: TyCtxt<'tcx>) -> Compilation { ... }
  }

  All methods have default implementations. Two matter for most extension work:

  - config fires very early, before parsing. You mutate the passed-in Config to install query overrides, swap the codegen backend, etc. This is your only chance to hook things in before rustc starts real
  work.
  - after_analysis fires after type-checking / borrow-checking, before monomorphization. You get a TyCtxt<'tcx> andcan walk the HIR, run validation, emit diagnostics. Return Compilation::Continue to
  proceed or Compilation::Stop to halt.

  after_crate_root_parsing and after_expansion are newer additions to the trait for intercepting earlier phases. Most consumers ignore them and rely on the defaults.

  Step 3: The config struct — rustc_interface::Config

  Two fields are relevant:

  config.override_queries    = Some(my_override_fn);
  config.make_codegen_backend = Some(Box::new(|opts, target| MyBackend::new()));

  Note the signature of override_queries: it's Option<fn(&Session, &mut Providers)> — a bare function pointer, not Box<dyn Fn…>. Your override must be non-capturing. Per-invocation state has to live in a
  static / OnceLock / thread-local that the fn reads; you cannot close over a &mut of your callbacks struct.

  make_codegen_backend is a boxed closure that receives the current SessionOptions and TargetTriple, returning Box<dyn CodegenBackend>. The target param lets backends self-configure per target; most
  backends ignore both args.

  Step 4: The query provider table — rustc_middle::util::Providers

  Rustc's compilation is a graph of queries — demand-driven, memoized computations each keyed by some input and returning some output. layout_of, symbol_name, optimized_mir, fn_abi_of_instance, and
  hundreds more. They are the backbone of incremental compilation.

  The top-level Providers struct groups three sub-tables: queries (the main fn-pointer table, one field per query),extern_queries (separate providers for queries with the separate_provide_extern modifier
   — i.e., queries that must answer differently for cross-crate items), and hooks (a handful of non-query extensionpoints). The override function your code installs:

  fn my_override(_sess: &Session, providers: &mut Providers) {
      // Save the default if you want to delegate:
      DEFAULT_OPTIMIZED_MIR.set(providers.queries.optimized_mir).ok();
      providers.queries.optimized_mir = my_provider_fn;
  }

  The idiomatic pattern: stash the default in a OnceLock, have your replacement either handle the input itself or fall through for inputs you don't care about.

  How keys and keying work. Each query's key type is whatever its Key impl says it is: LocalDefId for queries that only apply to the crate being compiled (cross-crate access is a type error, not an
  expect_local() panic), Instance<'tcx> for queries keyed on monomorphized generic args, PseudoCanonicalInput<'tcx,T> for queries like layout_of that also need a TypingEnv. Every key must be
  stable-hashable — its fingerprint becomes the dep-graph node ID used for incremental reuse.

  What overriding a query means for incremental compilation. Each memoized call becomes a dep-graph node whose identity is the key's fingerprint; the result hash is compared against the previous session's
   to decide green vs red. If you override, your provider is what gets fingerprinted — non-deterministic output (reading a clock, iterating a HashMap) silently defeats incremental reuse, and side effects
  you make outside tcx won't be replayed on green reuse. Write providers as pure functions of their inputs.

  The six queries this project overrides — each with distinct key, return type, and firing semantics.

  4a. optimized_mir

  ┌─────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │         │                                                                  │
  ├─────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Key     │ LocalDefId                                                       │
  ├─────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Returns │ &'tcx Body<'tcx> — the optimized MIR for the function            │
  ├─────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Fires   │ during monomorphization, once per function whose MIR is demanded │
  └─────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Return a hand-built MIR body and rustc's mono collector walks whatever you return. Call terminators queue calleesfor monomorphization.
  Rvalue::Cast(CastKind::PointerCoercion(ReifyFnPointer(Safety::Safe), _)) queues the target Instance for codegen even if it's never called — this is the mechanism for driving emission of a generic item
  you reference by name but don't have a call site for. (Note the Safety arg on ReifyFnPointer; older nightlies lacked it.)

  Constructing valid MIR is fiddly:
  - Statement and BasicBlockData are non-exhaustive structs — use the constructor functions (Statement::new(source_info, kind), BasicBlockData::new_stmts(stmts, terminator, is_cleanup)), not struct
  literals.
  - set_required_consts and set_mentioned_items must be called on synthetic bodies (normal mir_promoted doesn't runfor them; the mono collector panics if these aren't set).
  - Every BasicBlockData needs a terminator. TerminatorKind::Unreachable is valid for bodies that are never executed.
  - TypingEnv::fully_monomorphized() is a typing-mode flag (PostAnalysis typing-mode + empty bounds), not an input assertion — bodies containing ty::TyKind::Param placeholders flow through cleanly;
  rustc's collector substitutes them per caller when walking the body.

  Between MIR passes rustc runs rustc_mir_transform::validate::Validator, which enforces invariants appropriate to the current MirPhase — well-typed assignments, terminator/edge-kind consistency, cast
  input/output shapes, no critical call edges. Hand-built MIR usually fails here first. Run tests with -Zvalidate-mir set to catch violations the compile step misses.

  4b. symbol_name

  ┌─────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │         │                                                                                        │
  ├─────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Key     │ Instance<'tcx> — a (DefId, GenericArgsRef<'tcx>) bundle with erased late-bound regions │
  ├─────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Returns │ ty::SymbolName<'tcx>                                                                   │
  ├─────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Fires   │ when rustc needs the linker-visible name for a specific monomorphization               │
  └─────────┴────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Return any string via SymbolName::new(tcx, &s). Useful for redirecting mangled names to your own scheme.

  Important subtlety: computing a symbol name does not drive codegen. It's a pure read. If you only hook symbol_name you'll get "undefined symbol" link errors — driving emission requires something like a
  ReifyFnPointer cast in a MIR body (4a).

  Construct Instances with Instance::new_raw(def_id, args) (the plain Instance::new was renamed to force callers toconfirm they're not accidentally constructing an unresolved Instance).

  4c. layout_of

  ┌─────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │         │                                                           │
  ├─────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Key     │ PseudoCanonicalInput<'tcx, Ty<'tcx>>                                                           │
  ├─────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Returns │ Result<TyAndLayout<'tcx>, &'tcx LayoutError<'tcx>>                                                           │
  ├─────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Fires   │ extremely high volume — once for every type whose size/alignment rustc needs, including derived forms(*mut T, &T, Option<T>, tuples containing your type, etc.) │
  └─────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Return a LayoutData describing the type's memory shape. BackendRepr::Memory { sized: true } tells rustc it's an opaque blob — no scalar-pair decomposition, no niche optimization.

  LayoutData's current fields include fields (a FieldsShape with offsets and in_memory_order — the latter renamed from memory_index during the layout-data cleanup), backend_repr (formerly Abi; see PR
  #132385), largest_niche, uninhabited, align: AbiAlign (the alignment wrapper struct), size: Size, max_repr_align,unadjusted_abi_align, and randomization_seed: rustc_hashes::Hash64.

  Filter aggressively. You want to intercept TyKind::Adt only for specific types; intercepting derived forms corrupts their layouts and ICEs codegen. Check has_param() on the args to skip generic
  definitions that haven't been instantiated yet.

  4d. mir_shims

  ┌─────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │         │      │
  ├─────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Key     │ ty::InstanceKind<'tcx> — variants include DropGlue(DefId, Option<Ty<'tcx>>), FnPtrShim(...), CloneShim(...) │
  ├─────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Returns │ Body<'tcx>      │
  ├─────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Fires   │ when rustc needs a generated shim body      │
  └─────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Pattern-match the InstanceKind and synthesize a MIR body. DropGlue(_, Some(ty)) is the common target — you can return a body that calls a custom destructor.

  4e. collect_and_partition_mono_items

  ┌─────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │         │               │
  ├─────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Key     │ () (singleton query)               │
  ├─────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Returns │ MonoItemPartitions<'tcx> — a struct with codegen_units: &'tcx [CodegenUnit<'tcx>] and all_mono_items:&'tcx DefIdSet │
  ├─────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Fires   │ once per compilation, the bridge between mono collection and codegen               │
  └─────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Delegate to the upstream provider, then destructure and reconstruct the returned partitions — filter items out, or mutate MonoItemData fields (linkage, visibility) on items that remain. The LLVM backend
   reads data.linkage directly from the CGU slice it receives; it doesn't re-query mono_item_linkage_and_visibility, so post-partition mutations survive to LLVM emission.

  Linkage lives in rustc_hir::attrs. Visibility and MonoItemData live in rustc_middle::mir::mono. Getting the imports right is its own exercise; earlier nightlies had Linkage exported from the same module
   as the mono types.

  Useful for removing items from rustc's codegen path or forcing specific linkage decisions without a source-level #[linkage] attribute.

  4f. upstream_monomorphizations_for

  ┌─────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │         │                                     │
  ├─────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Key     │ LocalDefId                                     │
  ├─────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Returns │ Option<&'tcx UnordMap<GenericArgsRef<'tcx>, CrateNum>>                                     │
  ├─────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Fires   │ during mono collection when deciding whether a generic monomorphization should be linked from an upstream rlib rather than emitted locally │
  └─────────┴────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Return None to force local emission. Matters when generic items live in an rlib that doesn't contain the specificinstantiation a downstream crate needs.

  Step 5: The codegen backend — rustc_codegen_ssa::traits::CodegenBackend

  make_codegen_backend returns a Box<dyn CodegenBackend>. Rustc invokes its methods in a fixed order:

  1. name(&self) -> &'static str — identifier for this backend (e.g., "llvm").
  2. init(&Session) — one-shot init.
  3. provide(&mut Providers) — the backend can install its own query providers (separate from yours in step 4).
  4. codegen_crate(&self, tcx) -> Box<dyn Any> — walks the monomorphized items and produces CGUs of machine code. Returns an opaque handle.
  5. join_codegen(ongoing, sess, outputs) -> (CodegenResults, WorkProducts) — finalizes all codegen (blocks on any background threads), returns CompiledModules + incremental work products.
  6. link(sess, codegen_results, metadata, outputs) — invoke the linker. Note metadata: EncodedMetadata is a parameter here; earlier nightlies passed it through codegen_crate, but PR #141769 moved it to
  link (the phase that actually needs it).

  The useful pattern is wrap-and-delegate: your backend holds Box<dyn CodegenBackend> pointing at rustc's real LLVMbackend, forwards every method, and modifies inputs or outputs at whichever point you
  care about.

  Two intervention points are especially handy:
  - In join_codegen, append extra pre-compiled objects to codegen_results.modules as additional CompiledModule entries (with ModuleKind::Regular, object: Some(path), and whatever other fields apply —
  including links_from_incr_cache: Vec<_> which the incremental system tracks). Rustc's linker picks them up alongside Rust-compiled modules.
  - Around codegen_crate, run your own backend before or after rustc's.

  rustc_codegen_llvm::LlvmCodegenBackend::new() is callable directly to construct the wrapped inner backend.

  The trait was extracted from what used to be LLVM-specific code so Cranelift and GCC backends could plug in. An out-of-tree backend is loaded as a dylib exposing __rustc_codegen_backend() -> Box<dyn
  CodegenBackend> and selected via -Zcodegen-backend=<path>. The wrap-and-delegate pattern doesn't need any of that— it's just a trait impl in your crate.

  Step 6: Cargo-level interception — RUSTC_WORKSPACE_WRAPPER

  Not a rustc extension point strictly speaking, but relevant. If you set RUSTC_WORKSPACE_WRAPPER=/path/to/your-bin, cargo invokes your binary instead of rustc for primary workspace crates, passing the
  real rustc path as argv[1] and the rustc args as argv[2..]. Non-workspace dependencies get invoked with vanilla rustc, untouched.

  Useful environment variables cargo sets on the wrapped invocation:
  - CARGO_PRIMARY_PACKAGE — set to "1" only for primary-workspace compiles
  - CARGO_MANIFEST_DIR — the per-package source dir
  - CARGO_PKG_NAME — the cargo package name
  - CARGO_TARGET_DIR — the build output root (controllable from outside to share caches)

  The wrapping binary typically inspects argv[1]'s basename for rustc, drops it, detects primary-vs-non-primary viathe env var, and either delegates to plain rustc or dispatches into run_compiler with
  the custom callbacks.

  Step 7: The universal handle — TyCtxt<'tcx>

  Every extension point either receives a TyCtxt<'tcx> directly or can get one through the query it's plugged into.TyCtxt is Copy but its 'tcx is tied to arenas owned by the run_compiler stack frame —
  you cannot stash it in a static or smuggle it past the Callbacks::after_* return. All real work has to happen inside the callback or inside a tcx.enter(|tcx| …) closure.

  DefId vs LocalDefId is the related distinction: a DefId is (CrateNum, DefIndex) and can point anywhere in the crate graph; LocalDefId is a DefId statically known to live in the local crate. Query keys
  pick one or the other to turn "this only applies to the local crate" from a runtime panic into a compile-time guarantee.

  Useful TyCtxt methods:

  - tcx.item_name(def_id), tcx.opt_item_name(def_id) — item names
  - tcx.crate_name(crate_num) — crate name for a CrateNum
  - tcx.def_kind(def_id) — the DefKind (Fn, Struct, Trait, ...)
  - tcx.def_path(def_id) — structural DefPath; safe from any phase
  - tcx.def_path_str(def_id) — human-readable path; only safe during diagnostics, ICEs elsewhere
  - tcx.type_of(def_id) — the item's type (as EarlyBinder<Ty<'tcx>>)
  - tcx.fn_sig(def_id), tcx.fn_abi_of_instance(...) — signature and ABI
  - tcx.layout_of(PseudoCanonicalInput { value, typing_env }) — type layout
  - tcx.module_children_local(def_id) / module_children(...) — enumerate pub use re-exports and module contents, across crate boundaries
  - tcx.hir_crate_items(()).definitions() / .foreign_items() — walk the local crate
  - tcx.arena — the arena tied to 'tcx

  Query results that aren't trivially cloneable get arena-allocated: tcx.arena.alloc(x) returns &'tcx T, which is exactly the lifetime your provider's return type must name. Returning anything tied to a
  shorter lifetime is a compile error; use tcx.arena.alloc_slice or the tcx.mk_* interners for collections and interned values.

  Step 8: The threading contract

  Queries fire on rayon worker threads. The lifecycle hooks (config, after_analysis) and the CodegenBackend methodsrun on whatever thread rustc's driver is on. Query providers fire concurrently, so any
  state shared across them needs proper synchronization.

  OnceLock is the idiomatic choice for install-once immutable state (saved default providers, config). Mutex for anything mutable. Watch out for re-entrant locking — a query provider firing during a phase
   where your code already holds the lock will silently deadlock (0% CPU, hangs forever).

  Step 9: The nightly pin

  Everything above is #![feature(rustc_private)] territory — no SemVer, APIs drift per nightly. Pin the toolchain (rust-toolchain.toml with a specific channel = "nightly-YYYY-MM-DD") so drift is a
  conscious event.

  Churn is uneven. Stable across nightlies: rustc_interface::Config, rustc_driver::Callbacks's top-level shape, theexistence of the Providers struct, the existence of override_queries, the CodegenBackend
   trait's role. Drifty per nightly: MIR construction primitives (struct fields, enum variants, non-exhaustive attributes), ABI helpers (PassMode, BackendRepr, Linkage's module path), layout-data fields,
  individual query signatures. Budget ~1 week of adaptation per ~6 months of nightly gap.
